`jaraco` is a namespace package, and thus should not have the `__init__` (per PEP 420 and pip conventions). But if you look at the source, the file is there and it has a `declare_namespace` directive in it. It's pip that removes that file. You probably need to file a ticket with cx_Freeze to have it load the metadata for the installed packages to determine which packages are namespace packages.

Do you mind if I also clean some code for the non-async part along the way if I happen to find any? I won't make big changes. If so I'd make a new PR. E.g. in `connection.py`, `identity` function could be replaced by `lambda x: x`.

Now that we've made some refactorings in gabbi.driver the interface for this has become a bit simpler. What we need, essentially, is a callable that returns a list of dicts of the right structure:

```
[{
   fixtures: [list of fixture class names],
   defaults: dict of test defaults,
   tests: [list of individual tests]
}]
```

argument handling would need to be resolved but the simplest thing is presumably some kind of iterator. This would replace the cuts of `build_tests` which currently loops over a list of yaml file names and load_yaml on each one and creates a dict.

I'm struggling to replicate this issue. Can you send the output of a run of this when verbose: True? You should get two sections beginning DELETE that look a bit like this:

```
DELETE http://burningchrome.com/
content-type: text/javascript; charset=utf-8
accept: text/javascript

DELETE /
Host: burningchrome.com
content-type: text/javascript; charset=utf-8
accept-encoding: gzip, deflate
accept: text/javascript
user-agent: Python-httplib2/0.9.1 (gzip)
```

As you can see from my example the expected headers are being sent (that is, the override in this test are being used). If I remove the request_headers and response_headers sections from my actual test, the expected defaults are used.

Is there a possibility that your server side handler for DELETE does not have the behavior you are expecting?

Also, I can't help but point out: Your test here is representing a somewhat bad use of HTTP. You only ever need a content-type header in a _request_ if you are sending a body in the request (in gabbi that would mean having a `data` value). You aren't doing that here and you wouldn't often see it being done with a DELETE because you are deleting the thing identified by the url.

And another aside: While experimenting to see how things actually behave I was reminded that it is not currently possible to say that you don't want to use a default in a specific test. For example if I want to send a default content-type header in all of a variety of tests I can't say "no content-type header in _this_ test". This difficulty isn't a problem I'm likely to fix because though the use of defaults is perhaps nicely DRY for some cases it makes the tests less explicit and harder to read and it is named gabbi after all...

About the second point, it appears that cv2.warpPerspictive does not preserve the third dimension for single channel images. An example code that doesn't work on current master and works after applying the fix:

```
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from imgaug import parameters as iap
import numpy as np

images = np.random.randint(0, 255, (16, 128, 128), dtype=np.uint8)
seq = iaa.Sequential([
    iaa.PerspectiveTransform(scale=iap.Choice([0.0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.075, 0.10]), name='perspective'),
])
seq_det = seq.to_deterministic()
for i in range(16):

```

```python
iaa.SomeOf((1, 3), [iaa.Dropout(...), iaa.Affine(...), iaa.AdditiveGaussianNoise()])
```
would always apply 1 to 3 of the 3 augmenters (i.e. at least one).

```python
iaa.OneOf([iaa.Dropout(...), iaa.Affine(...), iaa.AdditiveGaussianNoise()])
```
always applies exactly 1 of the 3 augmenters.

```python
iaa.Sequential([iaa.Dropout(...), iaa.Affine(...), iaa.AdditiveGaussianNoise()])
```
always apply 3 of the 3 augmenters.

If you use Sometimes or augmenters with probabilities (e.g. Invert), there is always a small chance that none of them will be applied. This can currently only be avoided if you add some other augmenters of which at least one is always applied (using above methods).

`activator` defines whether an augmenter may be executed or not. `propagator` defines whether an augmenter may call its children or not. So the activator may allow an augmenter to perform its augmentations, but the propagator may then forbid to additionally call the children. Currently there is no real need for these to be separate, as every augmenter that has children doesn't do any augmentations on its own (e.g. Sequential only executes a list of child augmenters one after the other).
`preprocessor` and `postprocessor` are executed per augmenter before/after modifying the images. Maybe they could be used to log which changes a specific augmenter has performed. So far I have personally never needed them.
The main use case of the hooks in general is currently to use the `activator`. This is specifically useful when you need to augment sets of images in the same way, but also have to avoid using some augmenters for specific sets. Example: Augmenting images and corresponding heatmaps (labels/annotations). You want the images and heatmaps to both be affected in the same way by e.g. `Affine` (e.g. same rotations), but you don't want gaussian noise or dropout to be applied to the heatmaps.
For train/validation/test it is indeed intended to use 3 different sequences.

```python
# saved in test.py

from curio import run, spawn, aside, sleep


async def main(x):
  t = await x(sleep, 30)
  await sleep(1)
  await t.cancel()

  def test_spawn(self):
    # this works just fine, meaning no traceback
    run(main(spawn))

  def test_aside(self):
    # this leaves a SystemExit traceback
    run(main(aside))

Looking at the Gnome backend, it imports gi.repository.GnomeKeyring, and if that's not present, it's not considered a viable keyring. So it seems that PyGI must be installed for Gnome support. I also just noted that [PyGI appears to be deprecated](https://wiki.gnome.org/PyGI) according to their wiki. I guess that just means that keyring should use PyGObject directly, but that's a separate issue.

In general, you can test for the viability and priority of any keyring by querying the .priority of its class (requires keyring >= 2.0):

```
>>> import keyring
>>> keyring.backends.Gnome
<module 'keyring.backends.Gnome' from 'c:\\python\\lib\\site-packages\\keyring-3.1b1-py3.3.egg\\keyring\\backends\\Gnome.py'>
>>> keyring.backends.file.PlaintextKeyring.priority
0.5
>>> keyring.backends.Gnome.Keyring.priority
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "c:\python\lib\site-packages\keyring-3.1b1-py3.3.egg\keyring\util\properties.py", line 22, in __get__
    return self.fget.__get__(None, owner)()
  File "c:\python\lib\site-packages\keyring-3.1b1-py3.3.egg\keyring\backends\Gnome.py", line 32, in priority
    raise RuntimeError("GnomeKeyring module required")
RuntimeError: GnomeKeyring module required
```

As you can see on my Windows box, the GnomeKeyring module has no priority, so would never be selected.

Can you try installing the PyGI in your environment and see if it then uses Gnome Keyring?

I don't disagree that the behavior you described is insecure, though it's been the behavior of keyring since its inception, so I'm somewhat reluctant to change it for that reason, and especially as other use cases almost certainly depend on the existing behavior.

I invite you or others to comment on how to address this issue. One possibility is that a 'minimum priority' could be enforced, which could exclude viable but not-recommended keyring backends like PlaintextKeyring.

The problem is that `getargcount()` just looks at the method signature and if it sees `varargs` then it assumes that all variables can be passed to the callback.

A possible fix would be to use the arity value in the curried function as an arg limit when calling the wrapped function. Something like the following inserted into the `Curry.__call__` method:

```python
if (len(args) + len(kargs)) >= self.arity:
    args_arity = self.arity - len(kargs)
    args = args[:(args_arity if args_arity > 0 else 0)]
    curried = self.func(*args, **kargs)
```

With that patch and a slight modification to your example (the `_.reduce_(data, sum)` call won't work since `sum` expects an iterable but `_.reduce_()` will do `callback(result, item, index)`):

```python
import pydash as _

@_.curry
def curried_function(data, accum, id):
    accum[id] = _.reduce_(data, lambda total, n: total + n)
    return accum

ids = [1]
data = [1,2]
curried_function_with_data = curried_function(data)
result = _.reduce_(ids, curried_function_with_data, {})
print(result)
# {1: 3}
```

But if you are just wanting to sum the values in `data`, then you may not need to use `reduce_` and could just do `accum[id] = sum(data)` but I may be missing something.

Sorry for the late reply guys.

One of the reasons why I started development of puresnmp _was_ in fact to learn asyncio. But I wanted to wrap my head around the SNMP protocol first. Now that I have a working implementation I am absolutely in favour of adding asyncio support. This was also a reason why this was initially Python 3 only. 

One thing to be aware of is that #32 has now introduced Python 2 support. I accepted the PR for the time being, but by adding asyncio, Python 2 support must be dropped anyway again.

I was thinking of going as far as completely reverting that PR as it introduced quite a lot of churn due to the fact that type-hints are unsupported in Py2 and because byte-handling is very different in Py2 as well.

Reverting it would move us back to a cleaner code-base. For Python 2 compatibility a fork might make more sense. I believe it's possible to keep the bulk of the logic almost identical, so keeping the two forks in sync should not be too hard. Essentially the only I/O which is made in the library is in the `transport.py` module.

However, if `transport.send()` is made async, then this - as far as I can tell - will force all functions calling `send` would need to be async as well in order to expose this to the outer layer of the API such that clients can leverage asyncio properly.

I have not yet written anything based on asyncio, but I've done my homework and went over the asyncio docs. Any help is _absolutely_ welcome and appreciated. I will only learn from the experience :smile:

delay_factor and global_delay_factor really are generally intended to go slower not faster. 

For major tasks, like `send_command`, the method will complete once the trailing prompt is detected (or the expect_string if specified). The loop delay there is .2 seconds so it is a pretty marginal difference per loop (and the network devices are generally pretty slow on the CLI).

To me 4 seconds is fine...i.e. I care much more for every 100 users how many of them will have it fail or work (than is it 4 seconds or 600mS)

If you are talking outside of core methods (like `send_command` and `send_config_set`), then going faster, reliably is probably going to be difficult (note, I have tried to go faster in the past).

...that all being said, I am open to thinking about these core concepts and how they are implemented. I started to try to come up with a better integration in Netmiko 2 (but this is more for ease of use). Basically, I started to move towards timeout controlling the how many loops and the delay per loop in Netmiko 2 (where prior to this timeout in Netmiko really did very little). 

This was mostly so I could make it easier for end-users on how to adjust the settings when they ran into devices that failed (i.e. they need to increase how long Netmiko sleeps).

I am also probably open to allowing timeout/delay_factor/global_delay_factor (including with new concepts) to have things go faster...though the defaults will need conservative. Basically if someone wants to go faster and decrease their reliability, they can choose to do so.

I think the last commit pretty much implements everything @Hrxn's last paragraph hints at (even if it took me far longer than it should have):
- You can [select which post types should be scanned](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractortumblrposts) for photo/audio/video files to download (internally it requests information about all posts and filters the unwanted ones out).
- It can search post bodies for [inline images](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractortumblrinline).
- It can follow links to [external sites](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/master/docs/configuration.rst#extractortumblrexternal) (mainly useful for "Link" posts).
- Image and video URLs are transformed to their "raw" form (*)
  - `https://78.media.tumblr.com/ee589c6345f29d2d5935cecb49b0a705/tumblr_oztu02dIHp1wgha4yo1_1280.png`
    -->
  - `http://data.tumblr.com/ee589c6345f29d2d5935cecb49b0a705/tumblr_oztu02dIHp1wgha4yo1_raw.png`

By default everything should behave like it did before and only get images from "Photo" posts, but it is now possible to configure gallery-dl to get everything ... hopefully.

(*)
1. The SSL certificate of data.tumblr.com is only valid for amazonaws.com and is therefore considered invalid, which means raw URLs can't use HTTPS.
2. Roughly one third of all inline GIFs (and only those) yield a "403 Forbidden" when accessing them via their raw URL. Some work, some don't and I don't know why. Try `gallery-dl -o posts=text,chat,link,audio,video -o inline=true --filter "extension == 'gif'" http://setheverman.tumblr.com/` if you want to test this yourself.
3. Even "raw" videos have been (post)processed by Tumblr are not the original files that where uploaded.

> Some users have the habit to use the Link feature, which automatically creates embedded images if used in conjunction with certain sites (I can definitely say Instagram, and I think Flickr as well, probably more)

Tumblr even supports [Danbooru](https://mikf123.tumblr.com/post/167623351559) and [Pixiv](https://mikf123.tumblr.com/post/167624688109), which is really not what I would have expected.

Hello and thanks for using Pebble.

The reason the `ThreadPool` does not support timeouts is because it's not possible to stop a running thread. You can check at [this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/48540668/where-is-the-memory-leak-how-to-timeout-threads-during-multiprocessing-in-pytho/48545357#48545357) or [this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/323972/is-there-any-way-to-kill-a-thread-in-python?noredirect=1&lq=1) questions for more information in regards.

Only the thread itself can stop executing. The usual approach is using a flag to signal to the thread it needs to interrupt is execution. The thread checks periodically the flag to know whether it can continue or not.

Filtering would be hard due to the way the C callbacks work. I don't see how you could inject a UUID into the callbacks to identify which running bluepy-helper instance a response is meant for.  

I'm not sure the concurrent Scan objects on the same interface problem is worth solving.. I think that sort of works as expected if you run concurrent scans on say hci0 from two different Scan objects. But why would you want to do that? Though I should test that more for completeness, even if I'm never going to ever need to do that.

The big problem is Peripherals running bluepy-helper with the mgmt callbacks enabled with a scanning object running concurrently.  Those Periphals see status responses from the mgmt callbacks mixed in with the connected gatt device responses. And the peripharal _waitResp() call gets very confused and throws exceptions when the unexpected mgmt callback response from the scan gets shoved to stdout.  It's just easier to not turn on the mgmt callbacks if you don't need them for that particular helper process.

My fix is to not turn on the mgmt callbacks for the peripheral objects bluepy-helper at all to avoid them being injected into the stdout. It works great for my application.

here's the basics of my application
Main thread:
 1) Start up Scan threads for all interfaces in used  hci0-hci9
 2) Restart Scan threads if they fall over and die

Scan Thread for hciX:
 1) start Scan object scanning on each hciX  at regular intervals
 2) if Scan object finds devices named : VENDOR_NAME 
   start device thread for found device pass hciX as interface to use

Device Thread:
  1) Creates a Peripheral object and connects using hciX
  2) registers  notification handler delegate and waits for notifications with timeout
  3) checks for disconnect and attempt reconnect if necessary

I now have confirmation from Mert Bay (who was the first person in charge of multi-f0 eval for MIREX) that:

1. The offset criterion is as Brian (and the matlab code) interpret it: ``ref_offset ± 0.2 * ref_duration``
2. Unlike the matlab code (but in accordance with the verbal description), the offset threshold does use a 50 ms minimum, i.e. the distance between offsets can't be greater than: ``max(0.05, 0.2 * ref_duration)``

The current code implements exactly this, so we're good on this front. I'll look into squashing some commits into something more concise, and hopefully that'll be a wrap.

The metrics used in the aforementioned references are basically those used in MIREX :) Specifically, they're used in task 2 of the [multi-f0 tracking evaluation](http://www.music-ir.org/mirex/wiki/2015:Multiple_Fundamental_Frequency_Estimation_%26_Tracking#Evaluatio). It's basically recall, precision and f-measure given two sets of notes. Papers differ slightly on the thresholds used to when determining whether two notes are a match, but we can use the MIREX values by default and allow the user to change them should they want to.

This issue is actually more general: any init-declarator-list that contains a name that's previously been typedef'd will fail to parse correctly, except if the name occurs as the first declarator, for which there are various productions that act as workarounds.

I've been thinking a bit about how to get the parsing right while using PLY's LALR(1) parser, and I believe I've finally figured it out.

The essential change is to allow a TYPEID to be part of a declarator. We introduce two categories of declarator: (1) an id-declarator (equivalent to what is currently just a 'declarator'), and (2) a typeid-declarator, which is identical but uses a TYPEID rather than an ID. In most cases, these two get recombined into a generic 'declarator', which can be either of the two. In the special case of a function definition, its declarator must be an id-declarator (this restriction is valid b/c a function name can't have been typedef'd already because such a typedef would have happened in the same outermost scope).

However, allowing TYPEIDs in declarators obviously will introduce some parsing conflicts. To fix these, the other main change is to make a restriction to declaration-specifiers (and specifier-qualifier-list) which reflects two things: (1) it must contain at least one type-specifier, and (2) if it contains a typedef-name, it may not contain any other type-specifiers. This is required so that the parser knows when to switch from parsing a specifiers list to parsing a declarator. It's also consistent with declaration-specifiers as described in section 6.7.2.2 of ISO/IEC 9899:TC3.

After making these changes,  you can then remove the existing productions that are workarounds to deal with incorrect parses.

In summary, the changes to the grammar include:
1. Allow TYPEIDs in declarators
2. Restrict declaration-specifiers and specifier-qualifier-list to contain at least one type-specifier, and _only_ one if it is a TYPEID
3. Remove now-unnecessary workaround productions

I have not yet implemented the actual code for each production yet—I wanted to check if these modifications to the grammar make sense and if you had any related suggestions. If everything seems OK, I'll move forward with putting together the code, running the tests, perhaps adding a few new tests, and issuing a pull request.

This is intentionally not trivial and will disable plaintext authentication. Please make sure that you really have to disable STARTTLS / XMPPS support and there is no other way (such as key pinning, which is supported by aioxmpp).

*Iff* you absolutely **have** to disable the transport security, you can do that today with:

```python
security_layer = aioxmpp.make_security_layer(…)._replace(tls_required=False)
client = aioxmpp.Client(jid, security_layer)
```

(The ``_replace`` method is part of the namedtuple API.)

The ``…`` represent the usual arguments to the make_security_layer method. See the documentation of [SecurityLayer](https://docs.zombofant.net/aioxmpp/devel/api/public/security_layer.html#aioxmpp.security_layer.SecurityLayer) and [make_security_layer](https://docs.zombofant.net/aioxmpp/devel/api/public/security_layer.html#aioxmpp.security_layer.make), as well as the [quick start](https://docs.zombofant.net/aioxmpp/devel/user-guide/quickstart.html#connect-to-an-xmpp-server-with-jid-and-password).

If you want to use an existing TCP connection, you’ll have to subclass ``aioxmpp.connector.BaseConnector``, which is not really part of the public API. I’d invite you to discuss your use-case to see if we can find a proper solution in that case.

IPDB keeps objects in sync with OS using netlink broadcasts, and that allows to run IPDB regardless of what else is used to set up the network.

Unfortunately, unlike other RTNL subsystems, the TC subsystem doesn't send updates, so there are only two ways:

1. tc objects polling
2. place a requirement for IPDB to be the only TC manager at a time

So the TC objects in IPDB are not supported and I see no easy way to implement this support. If you have any ideas or proposals, you're welcome.

`value_serializer=json.dumps` is OK for many things, but serializing json is not very efficient: the keys themselves take up space.
If using something like an Apache Avro serializer, each topic would have its own schema. The advantage would be that you have both the structure predefined in a format (schema), and the fact that Avro packs only the values, not the keys. This is much more efficient at a network bandwidth level.

All things aside.. I think the proper implementation would be to extend the requirement for `value_serializer` implementation: always mandate two arguments: `topic`, and `message`:

Eg:
```
def serialize(topic, message):
    pass
```

it would still be 1 serializer... and whether the implementer uses that topic information is up to them.

There are 3 types of string indexes that rom currently supports:
1. SIMPLE/SIMPLE_CI - sorting only with `query.order_by('x')` - https://pythonhosted.org/rom/rom.html#rom.util.SIMPLE
2. IDENTITY/IDENTITY_CI - equality only with `query.filter(x=...)` - https://pythonhosted.org/rom/rom.html#rom.util.IDENTITY
3: FULL_TEXT - bag of words inverted index for query.filter(x=...)` - https://pythonhosted.org/rom/rom.html#rom.util.FULL_TEXT

To each of those 3 index types, you can further add a prefix/suffix index, whose semantics are as follows (prefix offers `query.startswith(pfix)` and `.query.like('stuff?*')`, suffix offers `query.endswith(sfix)`):
1. SIMPLE/SIMPLE_CI/IDENTITY/IDENTITY_CI - prefix/suffix the whole string case sensitive or insensitive
2. FULL_TEXT - prefix/suffix on individual words parsed out of the full text

Given your description, it would seem that you actually want `rom.IDENTITY` or `rom.FULL_TEXT` where you are currently using `rom.SIMPLE`, at least the way `rom.SIMPLE` is *specified* and built. If you actually need all of the variations of the index on a single column, you could duplicate the column data and have different indexes for the different columns, querying each one explicitly.

Or if you don't like to duplicate data, you can actually have additional columns with no data, but which generate indexes for other columns. This is actually supported right now as the `keygen2` argument available to most column types, documented: https://pythonhosted.org/rom/rom.html#rom.Column

```python
class RateLimiter:
    def __init__(self, max_rate: int, period_s: float or int):
        """
        Allows `max_rate` per `period_s`.  Note this implementation doesn't groom the queue (yet)
        so its memory usage will not decrease significantly

        :param max_rate: number of hits allowed per `period_s`
        :param period_s: period in seconds
        """

        assert isinstance(max_rate, int) and max_rate > 0
        assert period_s > 0

        self._max_rate = max_rate
        self._period_s = period_s
        self._lock = asyncio.Lock()

        # We'll initially allow `max_rate` to happen in parallel, and then release
        # the semaphores as new tasks can be started
        self._sema = asyncio.Semaphore(max_rate)

        # we'll push the task end-time to this queue during `__aexit__`
        self._pri_q = asyncio.PriorityQueue(max_rate)

    async def __aenter__(self):
        await self._sema.acquire()
        # by this point we can only have up to `max_rate` in parallel

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        # register finish time before we lock
        now = time.time()

        # Lock so only one task can play with the q at a time, realisitically this will
        # only lock for a significant amount of time when the queue is full, at which point
        # we can only process one task at a time anyways
        async with self._lock:
            try:
                # Start by pushing the time this task finished to the queue
                await self._pri_q.put(now)

                if not self._pri_q.full():
                    # If it's not full, we can immediately return and allow another task to start
                    return

                # Otherwise it's full so we need to wait until we can expire a q item
                # so we get the lowest priority item (oldest entry) and wait until it's
                # expired from the current window
                window_s = now - self._pri_q.get_nowait()  # nowait since we know we have an item and are locked
                sleep_s = max(0, self._period_s - window_s)  # min clipped to zero

                await asyncio.sleep(sleep_s)

                # we can now allow a request to take its place
            finally:
                self._sema.release()
```

@cuyu I have emailed the zsh mailing list with details of the problem and a proposed fix. I will report back with the resolution. If you wish to alter your own installation of zsh to fix the problem, you need to change line 11 of bashcompinit from this:
```
(( COMP_POINT = 1 + ${#${(j. .)words[1,CURRENT]}} + $#QIPREFIX + $#IPREFIX + $#PREFIX ))
```
to this:
```
(( COMP_POINT = 1 + ${#${(j. .)words[1,CURRENT-1]}} + $#QIPREFIX + $#IPREFIX + $#PREFIX ))
```
Note that this problem has nothing to do with unicode characters.

I'm hitting this issue also on Python 2.7. The problems seems to happen when argparse has an error in parsing the arguments to the subcommand's option. If there is an error, then the ``parsed_args`` is empty, and we don't get the values we need. We get an error in this case because the option expects exactly on argument, which it doesn't get. We can work around this by making the argument accept zero arguments with ``nargs='*'``, e.g.
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK

import argparse
import argcomplete
from argcomplete import warn

def MyCompleter(prefix, parsed_args, **kwargs):
    warn(parsed_args)
    return ['1','2','3','4']

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--parser')
subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest='cmd')
sub = subparsers.add_parser('sub',help='initial project setup')
sub.add_argument('--subvar1',dest='sub1')
sub.add_argument('--subvar2',dest='sub2', nargs="*").completer = MyCompleter
argcomplete.autocomplete(parser)
args = vars(parser.parse_args())
```

Then, we get 
```bash
./example.py sub --subvar1 x --subvar2 
 Namespace(cmd='sub', parser=None, sub1='x', sub2=[])
```
which has the value of sub1 populated.

Obviously we don't want to have to use nargs=* all the time; is a proper fix possible?

The problem is that you are confusing the platform.

  - The daily timeframe has a `time` of: `00:00:00` and therefore happens before the entire `1-minute` (it isn't *5 minutes*) corresponding date

  - That means that the `1-minute` stream is initially paused and the `1-day` bar is consumed

  - Because the data is preloaded in this case, there is a side effect when looking at position `0` which ends up looking at position `-1`, which is the last one.

Set:

  - The right time for the daily timeframe to avoid synchronization to be right.

Or

  - Don't preload the data

P.S.
  - The `1-minute` data would benefit from having the `timeframe` set.

I don't think so, I just replicated this with a blank project using only angular and django-angular.

My index file:
```jade
doctype html

html(ng-app="test")
  body
    div(ng-controller="CoreController")
      ng-include(src="'forms/user'")

    script(src="static/angular/angular.js")
    script(src="static/djangular/js/django-angular.js")

    script(src="static/js/app.js")
```

My app:
```coffeescript
angular.module 'test', ['ng.django.forms']
.config ($httpProvider) ->
  $httpProvider.defaults.xsrfCookieName = 'csrftoken'
  $httpProvider.defaults.xsrfHeaderName = 'X-CSRFToken'

.controller 'CoreController', ($scope, $http) ->
  $http.get('api/users/1').success (user) ->
    $scope.user = user
```

The key to replicate this is in the ng-include directive, so that the template can be downloaded and rendered by angular **after** the user model data gets downloaded. `restoreInputField` from django-angular.js then gets fired and overwrites the model data. To get more consistent behavior I put a sleep in my django forms `__init__`.

class enumvalueTypeSub(supermod.enumvalueType):

    node_type = "enumvalue"
    type_ = None
    def __init__(self, prot=None, id=None, name='', initializer=None, briefdescription=None,
                 detaileddescription=None, mixedclass_=None, content_=None):
        supermod.enumvalueType.__init__(self, mixedclass_, content_)

        self.initializer = None

Hi Patrick!

In case of standard libraries one has to be very carful when it comes to adding new features. Std libs are expected to be backward compatible with python version changes and it is more difficult to remove superfluous or not-so-well designed features. For this reason I think adding only the basic functionality is a good idea, later they can extend it if there is a demand for mature ideas. I'm pretty sure that those guys are good - and they are probably aware of alternative flag-implementations and can find out what features they have. Unfortunately I'm too busy with my life to become an active member of the python community.

In case of a pypi library (like this `py-flags` thingy) it is somewhat easier. I increase the version, I make a backward-incompatible change (e.g.: removing a feature), and ask the people to pin the previous version of the lib (if they haven't done it yet for some reason) till adapting their code to my new interface.

Cheers,
István

From electronic page 6, paper page 2:
> Floating point numbers must be numeric values. Positive infinity, negative infinity, and
Not-a-Number (NaN) values are not allowed in shapefiles. Nevertheless, shapefiles
support the concept of **"no data" values, but they are currently used only for measures**. Any floating point number smaller than –10^38 is considered by a shapefile reader to represent a "no data" value.

By default, the folds used are stratified since the default CV method is stratified CV. You can change it by setting the `cv` argument.

        cv (int, cross-validation generator, iterable, optional): Determines the
            cross-validation strategy to be used for splitting.
            Possible inputs for cv are:
              - None, to use the default 3-fold cross-validation,
              - integer, to specify the number of folds.
              - An object to be used as a cross-validation generator.
              - An iterable yielding train/test splits.
            For integer/None inputs, if ``y`` is binary or multiclass,
            :class:`StratifiedKFold` used. If the estimator is not a classifier
            or if ``y`` is neither binary nor multiclass, :class:`KFold` is used.

I don't want to raise an issue to you unless I can identify that the issue is in libtcod's C code on the latest master branch.  I'd prefer that my library doesn't cause you additional work.

libtcod-cffi now uses regression testing, so a scenario where you have to debug an issue with libtcod-cffi that I've missed is going to be rare.  If an issue does come up then the problem is that my regression tests didn't cover it.

With the regression tests in place, I'm free to do things much more insane then just using an in-between version of libtcod, since I'll know right away if anything is being broken.

If anyone has any issues with libtcod-cffi then I should be the one fixing those.  You'll then just need to worry about libtcodpy.

I think a good solution would to take [this unit test](https://github.com/HexDecimal/libtcod-cffi/blob/master/tests/test_libtcodpy.py) and set it up for libtcodpy.  I can make a pull request for this if you want.  You then add tests for any features you know can have problems.  Then libtcod-cffi can search for and must pass those tests during regression testing.  Running this test on both libtcodpy and libtcod-cffi is something I need to do anyway before I can take the current libtcod-cffi release out of alpha.

```python
svc_account = Account(credentials=...)
rooms = GetRooms(protocol=svc_account.protocol).call(roomlist='MyRoomList')
for room in rooms:
     room_account = Account(
         primary_smtp_address=room.email_address, 
         credentials=...,

     )
     for item in room_account.calendar.all().order_by('start'):

```

After hours of debugging, there is a trick to take care of when using both `django-cas-server` and `django-cas-ng` on the same project. The `sessionid` cookie may be overridden when jumping from `cas_server`'s login view to `django_cas_ng`'s one.  
With only the Django project on the CAS, it's transparent, but problems arrive when using a second app, because when it wants to login, it jumps directly to `cas_server`'s login, but this view's `sessionid` has already been wiped by `django_cas_ng`, if they share the same cookie namespace (aka the same domain name), so it asks for credentials again.  
It began to work properly in my case when I set up two domain names for the same Django project, one being used only for the CAS server, and the other for the website with the CAS client.

currently Gcal uses this method for editing events:

**Only this event**: similar to the occurrence edit here
**All in event**: this not only changes the event model, but also goes through and changes every persisted occurrence associated to the event
**All after this**: this truncates the old event to stop persisting at the time of the current occurrence, it then creates a new event from the new time and reassociates any future occurrences with this new event and changes them to all have the same time.

This would fix the occurrence doubling issues, but would also require a refactoring to choose which of these three options is chosen after clicking save in a new abstracted occurrence form.

For auto_bind:

Yes, having AUTO_BIND_NONE prevents TLS/start_tls.

This is the code in ``Connection.__init__``:
```python
if self.auto_bind and self.auto_bind != AUTO_BIND_NONE:
    if self.auto_bind == AUTO_BIND_NO_TLS:
        self.bind(read_server_info=True)
    elif self.auto_bind == AUTO_BIND_TLS_BEFORE_BIND or auto_bind is True:
        self.start_tls(read_server_info=False)
        self.bind(read_server_info=True)
    elif self.auto_bind == AUTO_BIND_TLS_AFTER_BIND:
        self.bind(read_server_info=False)
        self.start_tls(read_server_info=True)
```

and there are no other call to ``start_tls``.

From the content of ``Connection.bind``, it's perfectly legal to use ``AUTO_BIND_TLS_BEFORE_BIND`` with anonymous connections.

Thanks for reporting! Despite Django documentation examples using a list, most people have their `settings.LANGUAGES` as a tuple actually. Which makes sense, as this setting cannot be reliably changed after Django initialization.

Next release will split hvad settings from Django settings, solving this issue in a cleaner manner anyway.
Have to keep a tab on this though, to make sure it's not subject to the same caveat.

To be fair, when hvad started it would have had all Django features in "limitations". Including feature combinations, and there are lots of those (recently a bug was opened for a regression in combining `.select_related()` with `.values_list()`).
It grew slowly, features being implemented over time as they were requested (or sometimes pull requests got opened).

Otherwise you could do something like this (I find the subclassing syntax in Python pretty confusing so forgive me if this isn't correct):
```python
class binary_ufunc_primitive(autograd.core.primitive):
    def __init__(self, fun):
        self.outer = autograd.core.primitive(self.fun.outer)
        super(binary_ufunc_primitive, self).__init__()
```
This would probably give you slightly better performance, but at some point you'd also need to define the vjp of `outer` by hand, which is more effort/lines of code.

@dokato Populations and networks don't really belong in pylems. They're NeuroML specific concepts. This API should be just for creating pure LEMS. You can of course create something like:

    <Component type="Population" id="pop0">

but it's much better to use libNeuroML. See here for an example of using this to buid a network: https://github.com/NeuralEnsemble/libNeuroML/blob/master/neuroml/examples/build_3D_network.py

Also, it's better to separate out new Lems component(types) in different xml files and have all NML specific elements in other files, so each file can be validated separately.

It does have the downside you mention that updates to events.py will have to be ported into events_twisted, which is the downside that prompted this effort.

I'll make one further plea for the approach set out in the pull request, trying to address the points you've raised.

First, the pull request does not make twisted a dependency of soco (just as the approach you advocate also doesn't do this). Nothing is ever imported from twisted, unless the application which imports the events module from soco has already imported a twisted reactor. We could easily go further and make it an express requirement for anyone wishing to use events_twisted that they first import it explicitly, so that nobody inadvertently ends up using events_twisted (i.e. they imported a twisted reactor into their app, but nevertheless wanted to use a threaded event listener).

Secondly, the parallel imports you mention (events_base and events_twisted) have nothing sonos specific in them. Everything relevant to sonos remains in events.py. These parallel imports have only the bare backend for an event notification server (with a consistent interface). In one module, this is implemented with requests and threads.     indices = [0, 0, x, y ,z]
    mask = [0, 0, 1, 1, 1]
    z = dy.lookup_batch(E, indices)
    m = dy.inputTensor(np.array(mask), batched=True)
    z = cmult(z, m)

But there's still the case of accidentally clobbering one's existing `__init__.py`. E.g., if a teacher writes some YAML-based checks, runs them, thereby generating `__init__.py`, then makes manual changes to the latter, forgetting to change `.cs50.yaml` to point to that file, they've just clobbered their changes. I do think outputting of an artifact should be opt-in, else it's a surprise, documentation or otherwise. What about just adding, e.g., a `-y` flag or such that indicates "output `__init__.py` for YAML-based checks", which enables generation of the artifact in `$CWD`, else it's put in a temp directory?

It's a good question. I definitely think we should think about our broadcasting strategy, and I'm open to different methods.

One problem with implicit broadcasting is it makes backend work harder in some cases. For example, Caffe2 requires you to explicitly specify whether or not broadcast happens or not. You can't figure it out without fully carrying out shape inference and seeing when shapes occur.

Another possibility is an explicit "broadcast" op, with the intent that backends can discard it (if broadcasting is implicit) or use it to determine if broadcasting should occur. But should the broadcast op specify what axes/shape it broadcasts into? This might be too onerous for frontends.

Calling `pipenv run <unaliased command>` doesn’t operate relative to the project root for obvious reasons. If you are in a subdirectory and want to run a script you would want the behavior that exists. We don’t need Kenneth to explain that. 

Scripts however need to behave predictably. When you call a script you want it to run the same thing whether you are in the project root or some other directory, I would think. For example if calling a django projects `manage.py` in the scripts section I would expect that to work with a path relative to project root whether I am currently relative to the file in the same way or not
we are tracking this in our private tracker.

Forking commands into the background which themselves fork commands into the background isnt something you can assume is going to be a completed task just because the function returned. I said I know where you’re coming from because I do, I just happened to have worked on this in the context of our test runner which regularly had failures on windows due to concurrency issues. 

As far as hoping for a fix, we’ll probably merge #1538 today which relies on a backport of `TemporaryDirectory` and `weakref.finalize()` to handle removal of these files. Exception handling here would be an alternative but the implementation of `finalize` allows `TemporaryDirectory` objects to handle removal more cleanly, plus this will fall back on our own `ResourceWarning` automatically if it fails rather than raising an exception at all. Thanks again for the PR, and for the engagement on this, hopefully this helps explain where I’m coming from!

However,

> [...] will still make the same mistakes as before with requirements.txt vs setup.py. Projects will still be badly packaged with badly define dependencies in their setup.py because of that.

I share this opinion, getting new developer to successfully package their own Python code is actually complex, but it's not up to pipenv or any other to deal with that entirely. We could not rewrite the history. We, as a community, need to find a way to modernize the python tool chain, step by step.

And pipenv (and probably poetry) is a very good step forward.

Having to maintain `Pipfile` for application and `setup.py`for libraries is a no brainer. No matter how hard we explain with lot of words and long articles and good practices guides, it's too complex for what it is. My lib or app needs dependencies, I (as a developer) want tools to help me the more possible.

Their should be a way of extracting just the part that does the `requirements.txt/Pipfile`from libs such as PBR to propose a kind of 'easy setup.py', a Pipfile-aware wrapper arround `install_requires`, without all the unwanted behavior pbr brings, and package that in a dedicated setuptools wrapper that only does that.

So we would be able to have the better of each world:
- pipenv to maintain `Pipfile` (versionned in both libraries and applications)
- pipenv to maintain `Pipfile.lock` (versionned only for applications)
- one would use this magic wrapper package (`pipfile_setuptools`, `install_requires_pipfile` ?) that would be a first level dependency which job is only to inject `Pipfile`into `install_requires`.

I apologize for not responding to the original post. It seems to have slipped through the cracks.

> It seems that setuptools drop Python 2.x support.

Not at all. Setuptools declares and tests support for Python 2.6+. The continuous tests run against source and I've specifically run many late setuptools versions on Python 2.7 and 3.5 on a regular basis.

The problem is almost certainly that you have a corrupted environment (which can happen if you mix `pip` installs and either `setup.py install` or `easy_install` installs of setuptools).

You probably have a `pkg_resources` package around that still has an old version and doesn't have the necessary extern functionality. You'll need to inspect your environment and track down from where `pkg_resources` is being imported and why it doesn't have the extern package.

I can help answer questions as you investigate, but I'm going to close this ticket, as I don't believe there's anything setuptools can do to fix the apparent issue.

I think it would read better if you break out your commands into subgroups. Perhaps, something like the following command structure.
```
$ az sql db -h

Group
    az sql db: Manage databases.
Subgroups:
    replica
Commands:
    copy               : Creates a copy of an existing database.
    create             : Creates a database.
    delete             : Deletes a database.
    failover           : Fails over to a secondary replica. The secondary becomes the new primary.
    list               : Lists all databases in a server, or all databases in an elastic pool.
    restore
    show               : Gets a database.
    update             : Updates a database.
```

```
$ az sql db replica -h
Group
    az sql db replica: Manage database replicas.
Commands:
    create     : Creates a readable secondary replica database of an existing database.
    delete-link: Permanently stops data replication to a secondary replica.
    list-links : Lists a database's replication links.
```

```
$ az sql db replica link -h
Group
    az sql db replica link: Manage database replica links.
Commands:
    delete: Permanently stops data replication to a secondary replica.
    list : Lists a database's replication links.
```

```
$ az sql db replica link delete ...     # would break the replication link to the secondary replica
```

Hi @FrancescAlted, I don't think Blosc/c-blosc#153 is going to help at all with ``blosc_decompress_ctx()``. If I am understanding things right, that PR only changes ``blosc_set_nthreads()`` which is relevant only to managing the pool of threads used by the non-contextual functions. Calling ``blosc_decompress_ctx()`` never touches ``blosc_set_nthreads()``, but rather goes via ``blosc_set_nthreads_(struct blosc_context* context)`` instead, which launches a new thread pool for every context.

I think this is reasonable. We could change our stance to be that a suffix is added for the less obvious level arrangement. For example, if we had radiative fluxes on mid levels, those would be called e.g. `upwelling_longwave_flux_in_air_on_mid_levels`. I would keep vertical wind naming as-is.

What do we mean by 'last checkpoint'? Currently, this would mean checking for the last checkpoint in the rundir specified by the current script. There are various reasons a user might want to change rundirs-- those reasons may not always be coupled to where they want the cache to be. For me, since one of the exciting features of caching is that it would allow us to save work _between_ runs, it does not make sense to couple the caching to the rundir. I would prefer the cache to be saved in a separate directory. We could enforce a(n adjustable) maximum cache size to control how far back is cached.

I can reproduce the change in behaviour. It is explained by the fact that pre 0.14.3 releases would completely ignore lines with `# noqa`, whereas 0.14.3 recognises all the lines yet will silence errors for lines with a `# noqa`. The error in both versions only exists due to the addition of the `signals` line as both versions check `I100` for a each line against the preceding line.

I think the correct behaviour is to accept a `# noqa` on either of the two lines in violation of a `I100`, as you can argue that they are both out of order and ignoring either is ok.

**A more general response to "Why have a --user flag?"** 
https://stackoverflow.com/a/42989020/2220152
>pip defaults to installing Python packages to a system directory (such as /usr/local/lib/python3.4). This requires root access.
--user makes pip install packages in your home directory instead [within `~.local/`], which doesn't require any special privileges.

**My specific situation:**

I'm a user living only inside a docker container, and an administrator is providing me with access to it will always mount one single directory that will allow my work to persist in between container restarts: `/home/erik`.

To simulate this, you (as administrator) can run...
```bash
docker run -it --rm -v <choose_some_dir_on_host>:/home/erik  jupyter/minimal-notebook /bin/bash
```

Then, you as a user inside the container could run...
```bash
pip install scrapy
```

Do some work depending on scrapy, quit, and later come back, needing to do `pip install scrapy` again. But thanks to the `--user` flag provided by pip, I would get my `scrapy` package installed inside my home directory `/home/erik` and be able to avoid installing my package on an ephemeral location.

If you don't want to hard code all the packages (because you're vendoring so many things now), you can use this code:
```
import os
from fnmatch import fnmatchcase
from distutils.util import convert_path

def find_packages(where='.', exclude=()):
    out = []
    stack=[(convert_path(where), '')]
    while stack:
        where, prefix = stack.pop(0)
        for name in os.listdir(where):
            fn = os.path.join(where,name)
            if ('.' not in name and os.path.isdir(fn) and
                os.path.isfile(os.path.join(fn, '__init__.py'))
            ):
                out.append(prefix+name)
                stack.append((fn, prefix+name+'.'))
    for pat in list(exclude) + ['ez_setup', 'distribute_setup']:
        out = [item for item in out if not fnmatchcase(item, pat)]
    return out
```

Sure but I should not that fixing it requires asking the end user to set a registery key and that's going to be a lot of work.

Also allowing conda to install to paths requiring >260 characters will break a whole load of software with inbuilt assumptions of a maximum requirement of 260 characters, some of which testing would catch allowing us to fix but some of it not.

These unfixed ones can in turn lead to stack buffer overflows so it's not something that can be just done and forgotten about.

Regardless, a registery key with this option could be implemented into constructor and PRs to do that would be welcomed.

For cases 1-3, why is conda considering earlier builds of the packages to be equivalent to the latest builds? Perhaps it should consider only the latest builds (or at least, latest build metadata) when doing the dependency resolution? A package with a larger build number should be considered a drop-in replacement for any earlier build with the same version number. Alternatively, as suggested above, if there is a huge problem with the metadata of C 1.0.1-1, it could be forcibly pulled from the repo or marked deprecated or superseded in some way (in my mind, the existence of a later build is essentially marking earlier builds as deprecated).

In cases 4-6, how does Conda currently know to get the new metadata from the hotfixed C 1.0.1-1? The conda package C 1.0.1-1 I have on my system is *not* the same package that is now on the server (which is the confusing part we are arguing against). Could we apply the exact same logic of updating the metadata, but with the understanding that newer builds of the package have metadata that supersedes older builds?

Sure, `conda` doesn't actually care/know about those labels.
IIUC, a "channel" is either
1. just an URL,
2. a partial URL like `conda-forge` or `conda-forge/label/main`, `conda-forge/label/rc`, etc.,
   (which get `channel_alias`, i.e., `https://conda.anaconda.org`, prepended),
3. a "custom multichannel" like `defaults`, `local`
   (see `conda config --describe custom_multichannels`),
4. a "custom channel"
   (see `conda config --describe custom_channels`) \[TBH: no idea why this exists...\]

Thus, 2. is what you want and what you can specify with `conda >=4.4` in, e.g.,
```
conda install conda-forge/label/rc::matplotlib
```

@meeuw I really like this feature. It reminds me of a feature in MySQL Workbench, where there is a "SQL Insert" format when exporting. I can definitely see this being useful on a regular basis for its sheer convenience.

I do have some concerns though:

- Is CLI Helpers really the place for this? At first glance it seems like a database client feature, not a generic CLI export format.
- Because it's in CLI Helpers, we can't actually escape the values using the database adapter. E.g. https://github.com/PyMySQL/PyMySQL/blob/40f6a706144a9b65baa123e6d5d89d23558646ac/pymysql/converters.py#L76
    The postgres library has something like this, too.
- Unless I'm missing something, you can't actually run the result of this output because it uses the generic name `table`. If we put this in the database client itself, we might be able to get the actual table name in the output.
- I like lower case SQL, but it seems to be more industry standard across all SQL-like databases to use upper case.
- Does `NULL` get quotes put around it?

Most Scikit-Learn transformers and estimators assume dense input matrices. Therefore, the missing values need to be handled during data pre-processing, using the `Imputer` transformer type.

Currently, JPMML-SkLearn encodes the `Imputer` transformer type as a PMML `DerivedField` element. Ideally, if the `Imputer` transformer is the first element in the workflow (ie. operates on the "raw" input column, and all the other requirements permitting), then it should be encoded as the `MiningField@missingValueReplacement` attribute instead.

As for enhancing `DataField` and `MiningField` elements in general, then I have been thinking about defining an couple of new transformer types in the `sklearn2pmml` package. They would be called something like `CategoricalDomain`, `ContinuousDomain` etc, and they would take care of also computing the minimum and maximum values for a particular column, and keeping track of missing value and invalid value specifications.

Class `sklearn.preprocessing.FunctionTransformer` supports a limited number of 1-parameter [Numpy ufuncs](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/ufuncs.html): https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/blob/master/src/main/java/sklearn/preprocessing/FunctionTransformer.java#L79

As you can see, in order to support an ufunc, you still need to write conversion "business logic" in Java, and (re-)build a modified JPMML-SkLearn library.

Alternatively, send me the `/tmp/pipeline-pgh9sou4.pkl.z` file, so that I can re-run the conversion process in my computer (using the JPMML-SkLearn command-line application), and see the Java exception myself.

1. Create a branch at the commit before the change is added. This branch is typically named after the current version, i.e. if package version is 2.0.1, the branch would be called 2.0.x. In the future bugfixes can be added on this branch, while new features will be added on master.

The Pmp determination is the limiting step and it would be great to speed it up in any way.

Over the weekend I benchmarked some of Scipy's optimization routines<http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/optimize.html> for Pmp but could not find anything as fast as what we already have. The fastest was scipy.optimize.fmin_tnc<http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.fmin_tnc.html#scipy.optimize.fmin_tnc> and it was 50% slower than our method. I also implemented a dpower/dcurrent function based on Jain and Kapoor and supplied it to the optimization routines. If I remember correctly, the optimizer is about twice as slow if it has to compute the gradients numerically, though it varies a bit depending on which optimizer you choose.

In any case, I pushed what I did to my scipyoptimize branch. Here's the crux of it: https://github.com/wholmgren/pvlib-python/blob/scipyoptimize/pvlib/pvsystem.py#L1422

It might be possible to speed up that loop with multithreading or numba JIT. That scipy function should release the GIL and allow for efficient multithreading, but it won't make any difference if the functions that it's calling are Python functions. It might not be too hard to write the these simple functions in cython, though. The first thing I would do is decorate the function with numba.jit and see if it helps (see spa.py for examples, though it does a little bit of work first to avoid making numba a pvlib requirement). If that's all new to you, then welcome to Python's multithreading hell.

For Hadoop/YARN installations, client mode means that the driver is running on the Hadoop gateways, which are shared across all grid users.  For this reason, we generally don't recommend running heavy processes in client mode within our clusters, since it only takes a few "abusers" to effectively deny service to others.  Also, in cluster mode, it's fairly simple to request more than one PS node (albeit on GPU boxes at the moment), whereas this seems to just run multiple PS nodes as threads.  And, finally, have you seen any issues with running the GPU version of TF (required by executors) on a client/gateway w/o CUDA drivers?

After some debugging, it looks as an IPv6 issue.
Inside the docker, waitress will try to bind to both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses, but the bind on `('::1', 9999, 0, 0)` will fail with the reported exception.
But on the host waitress will bind to the IPv4 address only.

Yet
```
>>> from waitress import serve
>>> serve((lambda e, r: r('200 OK', []) and [b'hiho\n']), host='::1', port=9999)
```
this code will fail inside the python docker image but will successfully bind to the IPv6 address on the host.

It needs more research, but the issue might be on python docker image as well.

            preserve_default=True,
        ),

#### ``NewsBlogConfig.template_prefix`` (just a boring missing-migration bug)
```
        migrations.AlterField(

            field=models.CharField(blank=True, null=True, verbose_name='Prefix for template dirs', max_length=20),

The migration generated by Django 1.7.10 + Python 2.7 is exactly the same except the ``models.CharField`` parameters are rendered in a different order in the call to ``AlterField``:

```
        migrations.AlterField(
            model_name='newsblogconfig',
            name='template_prefix',
            field=models.CharField(max_length=20, null=True, verbose_name='Prefix for template dirs', blank=True),

For efficient indexing, `__getitem__` slicing would create a view if the sliced elements are contiguous in memory, or a copy if the sliced elements are not contiguous. It's determined by the fact that we only handle `NDArray`s with contiguous memory allocation in MXNet.

Operator slice in imperative mode always returns a copy like most of the other operators.

We can make it clear in the docstring of `__getitem__`.

Here is my implementation for SN-GAN.
```
class SNConv2D(nn.Conv2D):
    def __init__(self, channels, in_channels, Ip=1, **kwargs): 
        super(SNConv2D, self).__init__(channels=channels, in_channels=in_channels, **kwargs)
        with self.name_scope():
            self.u = self.params.get('u', shape=(1, channels),
                                     init=mx.initializer.Normal(sigma=1.0),
                                     allow_deferred_init=False, 
                                     grad_req='null', lr_mult=0.0)
            self.Ip = Ip
            
    def update_u(self):
        w = self.weight.data()
        u = self.u.data()
        
        _, _u = self.w_bar(nd, w, u)
        self.u.set_data(_u)
                
    def max_singular_value(self, F, W, u, Ip=1):
        """
        power iteration for weight parameter
        """
        if not Ip >= 1:
            raise ValueError("Power iteration should be a positive integer")

        _u = u

        for _ in range(Ip):
            _v = F.L2Normalization(F.dot(_u, W), eps=1e-12)
            _u = F.L2Normalization(F.dot(_v, F.transpose(W)), eps=1e-12)
        
        sigma = F.sum(F.dot(_u, W) * _v)
        return sigma, _u
                
    def w_bar(self, F, weight, u):
        """
        Spectrally Normalized Weight
        """
        w_mat = F.reshape(weight, (self._channels, -1))
        sigma, _u = self.max_singular_value(F, w_mat, u)

        return F.broadcast_div(weight, sigma), _u

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x, u, weight, bias=None):
        weight, _ = self.w_bar(F, weight, u)
        return super(SNConv2D, self).hybrid_forward(F, x, weight, bias)
```

This seems to be limitation of encoding. bzip uses BWT and thus wins from the input format (\n, and ' ' get grouped). Thus it gets close to conventional compressors limit - about 17 bits per integer; using range coding would have improved the compression ratio.
And of course, this is much bigger than true entropy (10 bits per integer).

BTW, if we remove spaces and new lines
`> ts=''.join('{:1b}'.format(random.getrandbits(1)) for i in range(10000))`
then 
`> print(len(codecs.encode(ts,'zip')))`
`1661`
`> print(len(codecs.encode(ts,'bz2')))`
`1692`
`> print(len(brotli.compress(ts)))`
`1262`

Having looked at some concrete examples of this - I'm worried about this change.  In isolation, I like the original suggestion.  But in practice, I think having `new` and `up` accept the URL in different ways is going to be very confusing.

With the current designs (as I understand them) users will think of `pulumi new` and `pulumi up` as being similar.  `new` gets you the code, `up` deploys, but they start from the same source.  In fact, most sources that are designed to be targets of these commands should support both.

So we'll have instructions that suggest either:

```
$ pulumi up https://github.com/pulumi/eks
```

or 

```
$ pulumi new -t https://github.com/pulumi/eks
```

That `-t` is very surprising in this context.

We're also generally moving away from `pulumi new` being just a "template" thing - the EKS example above is not something users will necessarily think of as a "template" - so suggesting to pass `-t/--template` doesn't "feel" quite right.

It may be that this points to deeper questions about the alignment of these commands, but I'm nervous about just doing a point change to the ordering of arguments on `new` without thinking through the full set of experiences here a bit more deeply.

Looking at `memorize_passes_needed` in `EvalFactor` I think I see the problem: there's some code that pulls out the set of names referenced in the code (using the `ast_names` helper function), and then reduces the `EvalEnvironment` to just those names (`eval_env.subset`). This was added in #66 as preparation for the pickling support -- it's why trying to pickle an `EvalEnvironment` doesn't try to save your entire Python environment :-).

And then if you look a few lines *after* we compute the set of variables to keep, then there's some code that rewrites the code to convert stateful transform calls like `center(x)` into method calls on a newly allocate transform object.

So I think the best solution is to swap the order of these things: if we make the list of variables to keep *after* eliminating references to stateful transforms, then it should just work.

It's not my call to make - but wouldn't it be better to store aliases in the Table instance (e.g. as ```self._aliases```) rather than in the DAL, so that a simple ```self._aliases.get(alias)``` would do?

Tables need 5 different table name properties because there are 5 different contexts:

1. Backend name without alias
2. Active backend alias (defaults to backend name)
3. Backend name *with* active backend alias (e.g. "rname AS alias")
4. Internal name under which the table is registered in DAL
5. Active internal alias (defaults to internal name)

The table object itself should provide all of these. The problem is that `Table.sqlsafe` is inconsistent and fits neither option which is the root cause of this bug. `Field` ends up needing 4 names:

1. Full backend name, including active backend table alias
2. Full internal name, including active internal table alias
3. Simple backend name
4. Simple internal name

All of these should be provided by the Table/Field object itself using the appropriate quoting methods. Building them outside the object (e.g. in the adapter) is asking for trouble.

The concern about Frame you brought up regarding low-level terminology is valid, and has been brought up elsewhere.  I started a discussion on zeromq-dev about this sort of naming convention in bindings, but it wandered off track without much useful information.  The only point that does seem well established is that ZeroMQ has multipart messages (i.e. Message refers to the collection), and it doesn't make sense to call the parts of a multipart-message 'messages' themselves.  Various names have been proposed by folks who don't like Frame (MessagePart, MessageFragment, etc.), but the only name actually in use anywhere is Frame (aka ZFrame, depending).

> I think this unified interface is the right way to go, I can't think of any good reason to split them up into three separate classes (with lots of code duplication as a result).

There wouldn't be any code duplication. My idea was to move everything into the base class (a single class for all dimensionalities) except for the convolution operation, which would be solved be polymorphism then. In Theano we don't have a `convNd()` function, so if we had a single `ConvLayer`, it'd end up including a lot of `if` cases in `get_output_for()` to distinguish the dimensionalities (where "a lot" means "three" ;) ). Plus, forcing users to explicitly choose between `Conv1DLayer`, `Conv2DLayer` and `Conv3DLayer` will avoid masking errors such as forgetting to specify a `channels` dimension for single-channel inputs.

I'm also unsure if it needs to be put in both add_param and Layer.__init__. I was also unsure of the who's responsibility it was to raise the error.  I think that it is necessary to have the error somewhere. Its very easy to create a convolutional network with too small of an input layer. That error should be caught when the layers are connected or created. 

The other option for where the error message could go is the output_shape property. It could check the output shape before it is returned. I don't think it should go in get_output_shape_for because that will be overridden and the error message should not have to be re-implemented. The problem with putting it in the output_shape property is that no checks will occur if get_output_shape_for is called. On the other hand, if the check is on Layer creation, then it is guaranteed to be called, and called once. However, I do agree that it should not be the a Layer's responsibility to check the integrity of its input. 

An alternative is that on Layer.__init__ it checks its own output_shape property. This is slightly different than the current state of the commit because the Layer checks its own output, once, on creation. I think this is the correct solution. 

I think adding a param and creating a Layer are enough different that the error should exist in both places. However, I've only been programming with Lasagne for a few weeks, so I could be wrong here.

Lastly, in terms of code readability, I'm all for keeping that as a primary consideration. I'm a buyer of the Tim Peters philosophy. However, without these messages, the error was only caught by a call to np.linalg.svd, and it was silent when Orthogonal initialization was not being used. Readability counts, but errors should never pass silently (or be caught by a cpu intensive singular value decomposition).

> T.shape(prediction).eval()

`eval()` won't work unless you also give it some concrete input, such as `T.shape(prediction).eval({input_var: your_input_data_as_a_numpy_array})`.

If you're just interested in the shape of the prediction, however, you'll probably want to query the output layer of your network: `output_layer.output_shape` or `network.output_shape`, however it's called in your code. This will provide the shape tracked by Lasagne while creating the network, as far as it can be inferred from the shape given to the `InputLayer`. If the `InputLayer` shape is incomplete, you can use `lasagne.layers.get_output_shape(network, (1, 2, 3, 4))` to compute the output shape for an input of shape `(1, 2, 3, 4)`.

@yoachim You can easily get the median of the pixel values in an aperture by converting the aperture to an `ApertureMask` object and then applying the mask to the data:

```python
from photutils import CircularAperture

aper = CircularAperture((50, 50), r=10)
mask = aper.to_mask(method='center')    # list of ApertureMask

data = np.random.random((100, 100))
aper_data = mask[0].multiply(data)
aper_data_1d = aper_data[mask[0].data > 0]
np.median(aper_data_1d)
```

You need to use `method='center'` in the `to_mask()` method to extract full pixels.  The other methods produce partial-pixel masks, which then require the use of weighted statistics (which you can do).

What's going on with the test failures is `test_certificates_parse_success` mocks out `certbot.storage.RenewableCert`. As a result, [this line](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/4155/files#diff-900252b9d61ceaebc9038ba9af425b58R76) sets `renewal_candidate` to a `mock` object which is then passed into `crypto_util.verify_renewable_cert_sig`. This function can't handle a mock so it raises a `TypeError` which is unfortunately masked by the error handling and the mocked out `logger` in the `certificates` function.

The easiest fix to this test is to also mock out `crypto_util.verify_renewable_cert_sig`.

As for adding a test for an invalid signature, I'd try using a different certificate for the cert or chain. `dsa_cert.pem` and `matching_cert.pem`  in `certbot/tests/testdata` both use a different subject key than the one in `ss_cert.pem`.

I've made an attempt at making this change. I'll be making a PR as soon as I've resolved anything that comes out of this discussion. You can see [my initial comments and a [largely outdated] draft PR here](https://github.com/peterbe/ciso8601/pull/1).


## v1.x problem
The problem with the current version of `cio8601` is its error handling.
In its current (v1.0.7) state:
* All valid datetime strings within the supported subset of ISO 8601 will result in the correct Python datetime (this is good). 
* Some invalid timestamps will return `None` and others might get truncated and return an incorrect Python datetime.

As a developer with a given timestamp string, I cannot predict a priori what `ciso8601` is going to return without looking at the code.
Fundamentally, this is the problem that I'm hoping to address.

## My v2 Invariant
In my mind, the best way to resolve this ambiguity (in the case of invalid timestamps) is to premise version 2 of `ciso8601` on the following invariant:

`parse_datetime(dt: String): datetime` is a function that takes a string and either:

   * Returns a properly parsed Python datetime, **if and only if** that **entire** string conforms to the supported subset of ISO 8601
   * Raises an Exception (ex. `ValueError`) with a description of the reason why the string doesn't conform to the supported subset of ISO 8601

My current work seems to indicate that this imposes a 10% performance penalty over v1.0.7 (but I'll continue profiling to make sure there's no more optimization to do).

## Conflict with `parse_datetime_unaware`
`parse_datetime_unaware` existed for the case where your input timestamp had `tzinfo`, but you wanted to ignore the tzinfo and therefore could save some cycles by not parsing the tzinfo characters.
`parse_datetime` already handles both tz-aware and tz-naive timestamps.

However, shortcircuiting the parse conflicts with the v2 invariant, as it is no longer checking the entirity of the string.

For example, if you had the invalid timestamp `2014-01-05T12:34:56ABCDEFG` and ran it through `parse_datetime_unaware`, it would not raise a `ValueError`, since it would stop parsing after the `:56`.
However, this timestamp is invalid and should raise a `ValueError` under the v2 invariant.

My response to this conflict was to drop `parse_datetime_unaware`. I've never found this use case to be compelling. Ignoring tzinfo on a tz-aware timestamp is almost never what you want to do (after all, there is a reason the tzinfo exists in the first place).

If this were truly what you desired as a developer, you could still acheive this functionality by explicitly dropping the tzinfo after the parse:

```python
    dt = parse_datetime("2018-01-01T00:00:00+05:00").replace(tzinfo=None)
```
So the capability is still there. It just removes the performance boost for this case in exchange for a consistent interface.

@thomasst [had the following comment](https://github.com/peterbe/ciso8601/pull/1#issuecomment-387625101):
> I'd like to keep parse_datetime_unaware. It could either ignore the timezone information, or only accept timezone-less datetime strings. 
But if we're strict here, should parse_datetime then reject datetime strings that don't have a timezone?

I'll now go through each line and rebut:

> It could either ignore the timezone information

This breaks the invariant of matching the entire string, as discussed above.

> or only accept timezone-less datetime strings

`parse_datetime_unaware` offers no value then. `parse_datetime_unaware` was only there for the case of better performance for timezone-aware timestamps.
`parse_datetime` can already handle both tz-aware and tz-naive timestamps without issue.

> But if we're strict here, should parse_datetime then reject datetime strings that don't have a timezone?

This would be a very bad idea. Both tz-aware and tz-naive timestamps produce valid datetimes. There is no distiction between them in the ISO 8601 spec.

Imagine that you made `parse_datetime` only parse tz-aware timestamps, and `parse_datetime_unaware` only parse tz-naive timestamps. 
This would then force the developer to know a priori which they are processing. If they were parsing a mixed stream of valid tz-aware and tz-naive timestamps, they would have to somehow implement a way of detecting the difference (by parsing the timestamps), which would defeat much of the purpose of using `ciso8601`.

## Alternative Invariant
Instead of dropping `parse_datetime_unaware`, it is conceivable that we could come up with a different invariant that addresses the v1 problem. 
I have given it some thought and have yet come up with a more useful one than the one presented above.

As a compromise, I would consider renaming `parse_datetime_unaware` to something like `parse_datetime_as_naive_unsafe` (naive [is the term that Python uses](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html), not unaware) which would have a different invariant:

`parse_datetime_as_naive_unsafe(datetime:str): datetime` is a function that takes a string and either:

   * Returns a properly parsed Python datetime, **if and only if** all characters up to, but not including, any potential tzinfo characters conform to the supported subset of ISO 8601
   * Raise an Exception (ex. `ValueError`) with a description of the reason why the string doesn't conform to the supported subset of ISO 8601

This alternative invariant would then be documented in the README. Something like

> **Note:** that unlike `parse_datetime` this will not catch all invalid timestamps. It will not raise `ValueError` in the case of an invalid timestamp having trailing characters (ex. `2014-01-05T12:34:56ABCDEFG`). Only use `parse_datetime_as_naive_unsafe` if you are certain your data doesn't suffer from this data quality problem (or you don't care if it does).

Note that the name change (`parse_datetime_as_naive_unsafe`) is done intentionally so that:
    
1. Developers don't assume that it is the default way to parse naive datetimes (that is `parse_datetime`)
1. There is a recognition that this function is breaking the (nicer) invariant offering perfect safety from data quality errors.


## Conclusion
I am completely open to suggestions for how to better address the issue. 

Finally, obviously you are the maintainers and I'll defer to your ultimate authority.

What you're reporting is actually expected behavior.

```
2017-05-17 13:02:46,662 INFO      POST http://x.x.x.7:9200/index_v0.2_2017-05-16_270b7c8edcc74744b39bbf304753b06e/_forcemerge?max_num_segments=1 [status:200 request:2056.259s]
```
So, it took 2056 seconds to finish the forceMerge.  That's not unexpected.  Curator [automatically increases the default client timeout when performing snapshot, restore, and forceMerge actions](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.0.4/curator/defaults/option_defaults.py#L214-L224).  It does not do so, however, when performing the other actions (except for that tiny bump for close actions, since it tries to do a synced_flush).

```
2017-05-17 13:03:26,934 WARNING   DELETE http://x.x.x.7:9200/_snapshot/force-merge/force-merge [status:N/A request:30.031s]
```
and
```
ReadTimeoutError: HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'x.x.x.7', port=9200): Read timed out. (read timeout=30)
```

Curator's default client timeout is 30 seconds.  Even with 
```
      retry_interval: 10
      retry_count: 10
```
...Curator will not ever retry if a client times out before it will attempt a retry after failure.

You'll have to either change the value of [`timeout`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.0/configfile.html#timeout) in your client YAML file, or set a [`timeout_override`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.0/option_timeout_override.html) in the action file in the options section for the `delete_snapshots` action.

It should be noted that the snapshot delete operation is still happening behind the scenes in the cluster.  Curator just doesn't get to see the end of the operation if the client times out before it's over.
