Here are more thoughts on this. Specifically, here is how user intents would map to changes in the state. Let's assume we have a simple document with `<CodeCell id="1"/>`, `<CodeCell id="2"/>`, and `<MarkdownCell id="3"/>` . The two state elements that we have are `editorFocusedCell` for the cell that has CodeMirror focused and `cellFocused` for the `Cell` that is focused.

Let's say the user starts off with `{editorFocusedCell: 1, cellFocused: 1}`.

1. User presses `down arrow`, the state is now `{editorFocusedCell: 2, cellFocused: 2}`.
2. User presses `up arrow`, the state is now `{editorFocusedCell: 1, cellFocused: 1}`.
3. User clicks the display area on `<CodeCell id="2"/>`, the state is now `{editorFocusedCell: 1, cellFocused: 2}`.
4. User clicks the cell editor on `<CodeCell id="2"/>`, the state is now `{editorFocusedCell: 2, cellFocused: 2}`.
5. User presses `down arrow`, the state is now `{editorFocusedCell: 3, cellFocused: 3}`.
6. User presses 'shift+enter`, the state is now `{editorFocusedCell: 4, cellFocused: 4}`.

There are probably other combinations of moving between editor components that need to be considered but I think this is a good representation of how the visual representation can be shown in the component state.

In my opinion, ideally, there should be a separate structure that contains the column cell matrix and state. The Connections object is currently being separated out to fulfill this role. (I also think at that point (of after) the state should be separated out, and the structure should be moved into a entity entitled "Layer"). Layers have a columnar/cell matrix structure, and the state should exist in the "Connections" object. 

After that the columns could conceivably be "primed" for or have some variable representing a depolarized state - since the reason for a single dendrite is to represent the cells in a micro-column having the same feed forward response. The "overlap" scoring could include a "boost" from the existence of depolarized cells in a certain column; arising from distal dendritic/synaptic activity.

If understand @fergalbyrne 's ideas correctly?

One idea I had was to have a formal "Parameters" object that can be passed around and maybe set on a top level, and then have them be "inherited" from that level down. Then have the ability to set a single parameter at a specific point (Region) and have that setting "copy the Parameters" in place, meaning that they parameter modification would not propagate to all the other Regions that shared it. A few ideas...

import os,time
import uiautomator2 as u2
from multiprocessing import Process,Queue

def get_devices_serials():
    devices_list = []
    fd = os.popen("adb devices")
    devices_list_src = fd.readlines()
    fd.close()
    for device in devices_list_src:
        if "device\n" in device:
            device = device.replace("\tdevice\n","")
            devices_list.append(device)
    return devices_list
    
def worker( serial ):
    os.system("python -m uiautomator2 init  --serial %s"%serial)
    d = u2.connect(serial)
    print(d.info)
    #这里可以添加自己要执行的脚本
    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    process_list = []
    serial_list = get_devices_serials()
    for index in range( len(serial_list) ):
        p = Process( target=worker, args=( serial_list[index],  ) )
        p.start()
        process_list.append(p)
    for p in process_list:
        p.join()
    print("all task done!")

@Rj7 the `TranslationDataset` class is not an actual dataset, and not passing the `path` argument (which loads the dataset) you are forcing the dataset to download itself (which it can't, because it's just a superclass for the other datasets in `torchtext.datasets.translation.py`).

This works in the test code because in line https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/test/translation.py#L52 the `Multi30k` dataset is downloaded first, and then the path it was downloaded to (default: `'.data/multi30k'`) is passed as an argument to the `TranslationDataset` in line https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/test/translation.py#L80

To summarize: 
- if you want to use the superclass `TranslationDataset` you need to pass the `path` argument to the location of an already downloaded dataset
- otherwise, use a concrete implementation `Multi30k` without `path` and the dataset will be downloaded.

A couple things about this: It looks like you are using SCMI files for ABI data. SatPy does not have the ability to read SCMI files directly right now but it is something that I want to (and will need to) add in the future. Any help would be much appreciated.

About satpy's performance and/or bulky-ness: code bulky-ness does not necessarily say anything about the performance of a piece of software. The SatPy code has also evolved over many years (starting from the old mpop package). SatPy also handles *a lot* of different cases and lets users do a lot of different things so it needs some complexity to the internals to handle these cases. If you have suggestions for things that didn't make sense to you or are very confusing, let me know or make an issue on the satpy repository asking for better documentation for a particular component. We have a lot of examples so I would hope that the highest level interfaces can handle most things.

As for performance, satpy depends on xarray and dask which allow us to have very fast processing code. The true color ABI image below was made with satpy in 8 minutes on my machine. This image uses the rayleigh correction from pyspectral, has been sharpened using a ratio sharpening technique, has been corrected for sun angle, and has been reviewed by multiple scientists to produce the best looking results we know how to make. Again *8 minutes* with a maximum of ~12GB of memory!

```
# Simple example using recurrent neural network to predict time series values

from __future__ import division, print_function, absolute_import

import tflearn
from tflearn.layers.normalization import batch_normalization
import numpy as np
import math
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

step_radians = 0.01
steps_of_history = 200
steps_in_future = 1
index = 0

x = np.sin(np.arange(0, 20*math.pi, step_radians))

seq = []
next_val = []

for i in range(0, len(x) - steps_of_history, steps_in_future):
    seq.append(x[i: i + steps_of_history])
    next_val.append(x[i + steps_of_history])

seq = np.reshape(seq, [-1, steps_of_history, 1])
next_val = np.reshape(next_val, [-1, 1])
print(np.shape(seq))

trainX = np.array(seq)
trainY = np.array(next_val)

# Network building
net = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, steps_of_history, 1])
net = tflearn.simple_rnn(net, n_units=32, return_seq=False)
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 1, activation='linear')
net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='sgd', loss='mean_square', learning_rate=0.1)

# Training
model = tflearn.DNN(net, clip_gradients=0.0, tensorboard_verbose=0)
model.fit(trainX, trainY, n_epoch=15, validation_set=0.1, batch_size=128)

# Testing
x = np.sin(np.arange(20*math.pi, 24*math.pi, step_radians))

seq = []

for i in range(0, len(x) - steps_of_history, steps_in_future):
    seq.append(x[i: i + steps_of_history])

seq = np.reshape(seq, [-1, steps_of_history, 1])
testX = np.array(seq)

# Predict the future values
predictY = model.predict(testX)
print(predictY)

# Plot the results
plt.figure(figsize=(20,4))
plt.suptitle('Prediction')
plt.title('History='+str(steps_of_history)+', Future='+str(steps_in_future))
plt.plot(x, 'r-', label='Actual')
plt.plot(predictY, 'gx', label='Predicted')
plt.legend()
plt.savefig('sine.png')
```

Should ldaptor API enforce bytes, or should it accept Unicode/text.

When running ldaptor under python2 there might be some places where Unicode/text will work due to the relax comparison in Python2.7

For example:
```
Python 2.7.12 (default, Dec  4 2017, 14:50:18) 
>>> u'text' == b'text'
True

Python 3.5.2 (default, Nov 23 2017, 16:37:01) 
>>> u'text' == b'text'
False
 
```

------

There are a couple of things that need to be agreed in term of strategy when the default behaviour of py2 and py3 differ.

For example:

```
Python 2.7.12 (default, Dec  4 2017, 14:50:18) 
>>> object() > object()
False

Python 3.5.2 (default, Nov 23 2017, 16:37:01) 
>>> object() > object()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: unorderable types: object() > object()
```

---------------

Since there are not many people with time to develop ldaptor and dedicate time to work at backward compatibility code, I suggest to look to the future and implement Python3 behaviour, even in Python2.

This might  break the Python code using ldaptor, but python2 will be gone anyway so the code should be updated anyway at some point.

But I am ok to go with any other option.

Thank you very much for your kind answer. I checked my case-study and the logic is correctly set to NRA, run it again in case I had made a mistake and the answer is still Unknown. The case study is copied below:

The idea is the following, there is a service that charges some money to processing information, the cost is formed by: 1) cost per megabyte, 2) cost per second (while the session is open); on the other hand there are me time constraints regarding the wait period to be served, the maximum amount of time to process the information per gigabyte and the network speed. Some of these attributes are functions that depend on time of the day at which the service is requested. Axioms establish boundaries for those values and the property says that there is bound for the total amount of money to be paid which cannot be satisfied because the amount of information to be transferred and processed is not bounded.

(set-logic NRA)

(declare-fun maxWait () Real)
(declare-fun maxTimexGb () Real)
(declare-fun perSec (Real) Real)
(declare-fun netSpeedMb () Real)
(declare-fun costMb (Real) Real)

(assert
        ;contrato de provision
        (and
        (< 0 maxWait)
        (<= maxWait 100)
        (<= 1000 maxTimexGb)
        (<= maxTimexGb 3000)
        (< 0.05 netSpeedMb)
        (<= netSpeedMb 0.15)
        ; Restricciones sobre costMb

        ;0000 hs - 0600 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 0 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 21600) ) (= (costMb tiempo) (* 0.5 (/ (perSec tiempo) netSpeedMb) ) ) ) )

        ;0600 hs - 0900 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 21600 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 32400) ) (= (costMb tiempo) (* 0.8 (/ (perSec tiempo) netSpeedMb) )) ) )

        ; 0900 hs - 1800 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 32400 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 64800)) (= (costMb tiempo) (/ (perSec tiempo) netSpeedMb) ) ) )

         ; 1800 hs - 2200 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 64800 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 79200)  )  (= (costMb tiempo) (* 0.7 (/ (perSec tiempo) netSpeedMb) )) ) )

        ; 2200 hs - 2400 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 79200 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 86400)  )  (= (costMb tiempo) (* 0.6 (/ (perSec tiempo) netSpeedMb) )) ) )

        ; Restricciones sobre perSec

        ;0000 hs - 0600 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 0 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 21600) ) (= (perSec tiempo) 0.01) ) )

        ;0060 hs - 2100 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 21600 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 75600) ) (= (perSec tiempo) 0.3) ) )

        ;2100 hs - 2400 hs
        (forall ( (tiempo Real) )
        (=> (and (<= 75600 (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400)) (< (mod (to_int tiempo) 86400) 86400) ) (= (perSec tiempo) 0.2) ) )

        (exists ((cota Real)) (forall ( (tiempo Real) ) (forall ((cantMb Real)) (< (* cantMb (costMb tiempo ) ) cota) ) ) )
        )
)

The most elegant solution would be for the library to do the detection for you, but still have the availability to directly call the individual versions if it was required in an edge case.  After all, dateutil is already performing a `try: import winreg` and duplicating that in each module that calls dateutil should be unnecessary.  

For SickRage's implementation this (along with #135, #142, and #143) would likely solve any remaining issues.

Having said that, I think there should be a single base class that handles all `tzinfo` functions (fairly naively like `tzutc`) in an OS independent manner.  For example, `tzname()` would just return the value in `self._name`; `utcosffset()` would return `self._dst_offset` if `self._dst_active` otherwise it would return `self._std_offset`.

Additionally, there should be OS specific classes derived from the base class that populates values with the correct OS specific information.  For the most part, I imagine, this could be accomplished through `__init__` and overriding property getters and setters, leaving the base class's methods largely unchanged.  These classes should all be able to reduce to the base class so pickling and unpickling can work in an OS independent manner.  This should also simplify equality checks.  Name issues like those in #144 would become a little more important for cross-platform compatibility, however after some initial checks with gettz(), it does not appear to have this problem, so maybe it can be leveraged as a solution.

Lastly there should be a public interface that should be called directly and which does the auto-selection of the OS specific class, but does not prevent direct access to the OS specific or base class, if needed.

I understand that this would be a fairly major rewrite, and you're the one to layout the roadmap for the dateutil library, but I think that the benefits would justify the effort.

I'm pretty sure that `_result` is in `parser` for namespacing reasons, not for any subclassing reason. `_build_tzinfo` definitely is.

I think this is not an unreasonable way to break things out, but I actually think that `_result` can be pretty easily replaced with `relativedelta`, or possibly `partialdatetime` when #449 is complete, which would be very useful, since then passing a `relativedelta` or `partialdatetime` to the `default` argument would allow `relativedelta` to be *returned* so you can see which components were neither in your default nor in the parsed string.

If that were done, the parts of `_result` that are not part of `relativedelta` (`tzname`, `tzoffset`, `ampm`) would be local to the `_parse` loop.

That said, I don't think that `_build_tzinfo` is a particular outlier here. Everything else is taking strings representing datetime components and turning them into constructor inputs, why should `tzinfo` be any different?

In addition to kinda going "too far", I also think this doesn't go far enough, in the sense that I'd like an intermediate result class that is *reusable*, so that we can cache the results. If the parser infers a given format, I'd like to know *where* it found each token. Might be that we first construct an abstract tree that *can* contain ambiguities, like this:

```
"20140304" ->
         %Y-()
           / \
        %m%d %d%m
```

Or something of that nature (likely we'd want to create some reasonable date parsing DSL that's more robust than `strptime`'s). Downstream consumers who want unusual control over their datetime parsing can manipulate the intermediate syntax tree, and we can cache it so that later parses are fast.

It's probably premature to separate responsibilities out of `parser`.  But suppose for the moment that you decided it was worthwhile (say a bunch of new parsing methods are added and the class gets larger than you like).  In this context, a reasonable separation of responsibilities if "everything directly dealing with parsing the string" and "everything after that".

(Note: acknowledging that the refactoring is premature is part of an effort to meet you halfway on this)

Latest thoughts on how we can deal with this.

1. Change `FastChainSyncer` to first download the header chain (no block bodies or receipts)
2. Run both the `FastChainSyncer` and the `StateDownloader` concurrently once the header chain has been synced.
3. Anytime the state root that the `StateDownloader` is using is more than X blocks behind the current head of the chain, update to a newer state root.

We may need to build a mechanism for *smooth* transition from one state root to another in the state downloader.  Simply re-starting the state download with the new state root will potentially incur significant overhead.  Ideally we can preserve whatever data from the existing state downloader and populate the new one, filtering out any `SyncRequest` that is for a node-key which is not present under the new state root.

One example is for automating the primary analysis. Janez will prepare certain data objects on the platform (genome, gene model, ontology...) and then in the configuration specify them as inputs to the pipeline.

Another example is that we wish to make the GO Enrichment Analysis process easy to use. Based on the inputs it can figure out which annotation files it should use (human or mouse). The process would expect either human-gaf or mouse-gaf data object on the platform. If they would not be available, it would fail.

Specifically, when you run the GO Enrichment Analysis all that is really needed on input are gene IDs. We can then figure out automatically if the IDs are mouse or human. Based on this we can find the appropriate GAF on the platform automatically, so there is no need for the user to specify it.

There may be more to this than just `hupcl`, unfortunately.

For instance, running the following code:

	stty -a -F /dev/ttyACM0 | grep --only-matching -- '-hupcl' ; sleep 10; python3 -c 'from time import sleep;fd=open("/dev/ttyACM0");sleep(8)' & (sleep 3; tty -a -F /dev/ttyACM0 | grep --only-matching -- '-hupcl')

which:

* Checks the status of `hupcl` on `/dev/ttyACM0`
* (Waits 10s for the device to finish rebooting after a potential reset)
* Opens the device using `open` in Python,
*and, while it's open:*
  * Waits 3 seconds
  * Checks the status of `hupcl` again
  * Waits 5 more seconds, then
* Closes it in Python using `close`

When I ran it, reset the device during Python's `open` **despite `-hupcl` being output _twice_** (once for each check: the pre-check, and the check while Python was holding the file open.)

So, this patch will probably not fix #124 in any case. While it may be necessary, it seems it's certainly not sufficient to disable `HUPCL` to fix that bug.

i would not use such short timeouts, only in the range of multiple seconds.

if sending byte-by-byte works better, suggests that the microcontroller is not fast enough at receiving the bytes. maybe using two stop bits helps, tough that only gives the controller only marginally more time. consider using a lower baud rate or optimize the code.

its also possible that the connection requires hardware flow control.

@singingwolfboy @jorwoods I just posted issue 324 which is similar to this, but within fetch_access_token rather than refresh_token. 

I hope I provided enough context so we all can better understand what is going on.

Recently playing with our internal OAuth provider and inspecting the logs I noticed the following message: `Prepared refresh token request body grant_type=refresh_token&scope=0-0-0-0-0&refresh_token=XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
&allow_redirects=True`. The last `allow_redirects=True` pair is included because `requests.sessions.Session#get()` sets it on by default, and, as it was discussed above, all extra `**kwargs` of the `request()` method are passed to `refresh_token()`. As a result, basically any keyword argument of `get()`, `post()`, etc., like `params` or `headers`, besides those explicitly declared in `refresh_token()` is included in the body of auto refresh request. I agree that having a dedicated a parameter that allows to specify which extra arguments should be passed to `refresh_token()` would be a proper general-case solution. But for the time being, at least not including arbitrary parameters of request methods in the body of auto refresh seems like a reasonable workaround.

> I think a function (duecredit_cite) should suffice

Note that Matlab / Octave do not have direct import functionality. Thus, functions using duecredit would have to call that function directly. But since it may not be present on other machines, every time it is to be used it should be surrounded by try / catch, e.g.:

    try
        duecredit_cite('GNU Octave')
    catch
        % do nothing
    end

which is not very elegant...

> I am scared of thinking OOP in Matlab/Octave but if you know some compatible way which will withstand the test of time -- sure ;)

OOP will be difficult, particular if this is to be Octave compatible. Octave only supports 'old-style' OOP, which means syntax like this:

    duecredit = cite(duecredit, 'GNU Octave')

It seems that a function using a persistent variable is easiest.

> Also I wonder how easy would be to enable/disable (nothing to be done at all by duecredit_cite) the mode observing "DUECREDIT_ENABLE" env variable?

That should be straightforward, as matlab has a ``getenv`` function.

> Supporting formats could be left to the "duecredit core" which is what we are writing here, and which will support all that madness. So Matlab side would just need to collect those references (plain strings for bibtex or doi or url), save into some file (e.g. .duecredit.mat) which we would load in duecredit, process and add to the global list of citations.

Does that mean that duecredit for Matlab/ Octave would always require that Python and the Python code for duecredit is available? Many users of Matlab run on MS Windows and are unlikely to have that available, or may not be willing to invest the time setting that up... Alternatively, a pure Matlab / Octave implementation may be an option, but that involves a lot of code and effort duplication.


Also, I looked into the injector functionality. That's nice, but more difficult to achieve on Matlab / Octave. The only possible way I could see to make this work is have subdirectories for each package that have the same name as functions called by the respective package (such as ft_defaults for FieldTrip). Upon duecredit initalization, these subdirectories are added to the top of the search path, overriding the original function. Upon the first call of such a function, duecredit_cite is called and the directory removed from the search path. However, such a solution is not very scalable (certain toolboxes have many functions that may be called), and also not elegant as it involves run-time modifications to the search path. It also does not work if a toolbox function is called from the toolbox directory itself, as the current directory has higher precedence than anything in the search path. 
Thus, any ideas on how injector functionality can be achieved would be appreciated.

I think it's a documentation issue. I followed the quick installation instructions on my sentry instance, which suggests this line

    'release': raven.fetch_git_sha(os.path.dirname(__file__)),

in your `settings.py`. But if your `settings.py` is like mine one folder down from your git root folder, then you get the error you describe (because of this: https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/versioning.py#L22). Instead follow https://docs.sentry.io/clients/python/integrations/django/ and replace the line with:

    'release': raven.fetch_git_sha(os.path.dirname(os.pardir)),

and you're in business!

Bonjour @xtrembaker, et merci de nous avoir signalé ce problème.

> n'avez-vous pas de logique de versionning pour éviter ce genre de désagrément ?

OpenFisca est un produit qui évolue beaucoup en ce moment, y compris de manière non rétro-compatible. Nous avons une politique de [semantic versioning](http://semver.org/) stricte sur tous les packages pour nous prémunir de ce genre de désagrément.

En revanche, il n'y a aujourd'hui aucune garantie de stabilité sur l'API publique de démonstration `api.openfisca.fr`. Les ré-utilisateurs d'OpenFisca qui ont des applications en production ont aujourd'hui [leur propre instance d'OpenFisca](https://doc.openfisca.fr/openfisca-web-api/install_an_api_instance.html), et peuvent ainsi [garder le contrôle sur la version servie](https://github.com/sgmap/mes-aides-ui/blob/master/openfisca/requirements.txt).

> je ne comprends pas pourquoi dans certains cas il faut passer un objet en précisant l'année....

Il y a sans doute un effort de documentation à faire sur ce point. OpenFisca gère des situations qui évoluent dans le temps. Quand on déclare une variable en entrée, on le fait toujours pour une période précise. 

Si on ne précise pas de période, par exemple en envoyant `"enfant_a_charge": false`, alors c'est la période globale (ici `2017-02`) qui est retenue. Or `enfant_a_charge` est une variable aujourd'hui liée à la déclaration d'impôts, donc annuelle. Nous avons rendu récemment le moteur plus strict, et il refuse aujourd'hui que l'on assigne une valeur mensuelle à une variable annuelle. D'où la nécessité de préciser le `2017`.

La valeur par défaut et le type n'ont pas d'impact sur ceci. Quels qu'ils soient, la structure de la déclaration d'un input dans le JSON est toujours la même:

```JSON
nom_de_la_variable: {
  "2017-01": 1000,
  "2017-02": 1000,
  "2017-03": "..."
  }
```
La syntaxe :
```json
nom_de_la_variable: 1000
```

n'est qu'un raccourci pour 

```json
nom_de_la_variable: {"2017-02": 1000}
```

You nee to make sure that `&ambiwidth` Vim option has correct value, identical to the similar iTerm2 option. If this is false Vim cannot know width of the status line and thus cannot truncate correctly, same problem applies to powerline.

Note that any kind of multiline statusline is a Vim issue (or your Vim configuration). Normally if powerline for some reason produced too long status line Vim will simply truncate it. It cannot do this only when it has computed statusline width wrongly.

**For SUSE and openSUSE**

```
$ sudo zypper update && zypper install rng-tools python-devel libffi-devel gcc python2-pip libopenssl-devel
$ sudo pip2 install blockstack --upgrade

Suggestion for General Post-Install Setup Instructions:

```
$ blockstack setup
<ENTER A NEW PASSWORD>
```

On the above step, I would suggest:

1. *obfuscate password* on "Got backup?" query; do not print out on terminal.
2. Note to user that config files have been installed to `$HOME/.blockstack`
3. Note to user that 'wallet.json' file in `$HOME/.blockstack/` contains public key, payment address, and 'enc' (encrypted private key, I presume?)

Following `$ blockstack setup`, start the api, entering your password when prompted:

```
$ blockstack api start
Enter wallet password:
{

}
```

Next run `$ blockstack info` to test and verify your setup.

The plist we delived was  based on a sample package list on Githup.

I duly reported that it could not possible work and why: We cannot assign
OTL sources to UI masters because there are no UI masters. OTL sources
belong to interpolation coordinates of font families as expressed by
instances, that make use of masters. I made a proposal how a package list
could be made to work.

The plist that is at the link, is not what I make: We delivered one with
the UI and document definitions in a single file and without the cmap keys
being added. The addition of cmap references is a step forward, but as
long as you are trying to make fonts from masters that do not exist it is
bound to fail. In Glyphs sources where there is one font family only, the
plist format may appear to work. In that situation the assumption that
master equals font source works. But in Glyphs files masters and fonts
(instances) are separated and masters can be used as source for several
font families. Therefore the software to generate fonts from Glyphs
sources has to respect the hierarchy of the file format.

@iulianav So, with all the additional discussion, we're changing our target spec, like so:

- names should be normalized by using diacritics expansion, thankfully, `unicodedata` provides a `normalize` method.  
As we discussed with @jacquerie, my suggestion (along with some introduction in unicode normalization forms) is this:  

  Regarding unicode normalization we have `NF` (normalization form) `C`(omposed) OR `D`(ecomposed).  
The `C` means that it’s going to combine as much as possible code points into one, while `D` means it’s going to use many different codepoints (e.g. for an accented letter it's going to use 2 code points).

  **=> 1st choice: going for the `NFC`** - as it makes sense to use the least possible bytes to represent the same letters.

  Then we need to choose between `NFC` and `NFKC`, which work into two phases A) decomposition and B) composition.
Both are doing canonical composition, but NFC does “Canonical Decomposition”, while NFKC does “Compatibility Decomposition”. 

  Elaboration from ElasticSearch documentation:
  > The canonical forms — `nfc` and `nfd` — represent ligatures like `ﬃ` or `œ` as a single character, while the compatibility forms — `nfkc` and `nfkd` — break down these composed characters into a simpler multiletter equivalent: `f` + `f` + `i` or `o` + `e`.

  **=> 2nd choice: NFKC.**

- Also, we have to do lowercase normalization in our tokens.
- While finally, making sure that dots in the end (or their absense) return the same consistent results (bug in legacy).

**The end goal would be to do term queries on an author field with the user's input.** 

Sources:
[1] http://unicode.org/reports/tr15/ 
[2] https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/guide/current/unicode-normalization.html

I was stuck with the same problem: in my case, not even "standard" attributes like "low", "high", "y" would work when creating the data list "by hand". I then figured out that the problem was with my datatypes, i.e. timedate-like values. I got errors like:

    series_data = Series(data, series_type=series_type, **kwargs)
  File "/home/francesca/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/highcharts/highcharts/highchart_types.py", line 629, in __init__
    if SeriesOptions.__validate_options__(k,v,DATA_SERIES_ALLOWED_OPTIONS[k]):
  File "/home/francesca/anaconda3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/highcharts/highcharts/highchart_types.py", line 512, in __validate_options__
    raise OptionTypeError("Option Type Currently Not Supported: %s" % k)

I temporarily solved this by commenting out the line setting the accepted types for 'x' in DATA_SERIES_ALLOWED_OPTIONS.  Now both standard and custom attributes to data work fine. This is clearly just a patch and by no means intended to be the final solutions, but it shows that custom data attributes are supported already. Hope it helps.

`--constraints` in pip constrains the versions of libraries if, and only if, they are required by the package or one of its dependencies. An example would probably help here; given a file:

    # constraints.txt
    requests==2.0.0
    dontyouwantmebaby==0.0.0

pip will find and install (or just download in this example) only requests==2.0.0:

    $ pip download --constraint constraints.txt requests
    Collecting requests==2.0.0 (from -c constraints.txt (line 1))
      File was already downloaded /Users/john.harrison/code/pex/requests-2.0.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
    Successfully downloaded requests

whereas pex attempts to satisfy a non-requirement:

    $ ./dist/pex27 --constraints constraints.txt requests
    Could not satisfy all requirements for dontyouwantmebaby==0.0.0:

I was about to post something about no longer being confident that we can have a "Snapshot by default" API, but while I was typing that I remembered a dumb thing about OSX's tar implementation.

After running:
```
./pants filedeps --sep='\0' :: > filelist-null.txt
```

...the following operations take the following time:
```
cat filelist-null.txt | COPYFILE_DISABLE=1 time xargs -0 tar -n -cf - > /dev/null
    14.02    seconds
cat filelist-null.txt | COPYFILE_DISABLE=1 time xargs -0 -P8 tar -n -cf - > /dev/null
    17.06    seconds
cat filelist-null.txt | time xargs -0 tar -n -cf - > /dev/null
    189.63  seconds
cat filelist-null.txt | time xargs -0 -P8 tar -n -cf - > /dev/null
    110.15   seconds
cat filelist-null.txt | time xargs -0 sha1sum > /dev/null
    7.09     seconds
cat filelist-null.txt | time xargs -0 -P8 sha1sum > /dev/null
    11.61    seconds
```

So it looks like Snapshot-by-default is still feasible. The primary concern will be that we will need to figure out how to garbage collect snapshots... for now, we should be able to create them as nested temporary files, and only later worry about shipping them places.

I can hold off.  I have a local workaround Teodor can try.  I mistakenly entered it on #940 though!  Here it is:

A short-term workaround is to uninstall the android backend manually.  This would require adding a local pants plugin like so:

1. edits to pants.ini
 ```ini
[DEFAULT]
pythonpath: ["%(buildroot)s/pants"]

[backends]
packages: ["custom"]
 ```

2. new dir/file `mkdir -p pants/custom && touch pants/custom/__init__.py`

3. new file pants/custom/register.py
 ```python
import inspect
import logging

from pants.goal.goal import Goal


logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def register_goals():
  uninstall_android_tasks()


def uninstall_android_tasks():
  for goal in Goal.all():
    for name in goal.ordered_task_names():
      task_type = goal.task_type_by_name(name)
      for clazz in inspect.getmro(task_type):
        if clazz.__module__.startswith('pants.backend.android'):
          logger.debug('Uninstalling task {} {}'.format(name, task_type))
          goal.uninstall_task(name)
          break
 ```

And then you should see something like this:
```console

Hey folks.

We have a workaround for this currently which uses gunicorn's [*child_exit*](http://docs.gunicorn.org/en/stable/settings.html#child-exit) hook to merge the dead worker's counter/histogram metrics into archive files and deletes the pid file. This has kept our file count and thus scrape response times stable. The attached image shows our scrape times (yellow line) when we deployed our workaround.

Our thinking was that using *child_exit* avoids the need to lock, as it's run only in the master. We still call the current *mark_process_dead()* function too, so gauge files are removed (although we are not heavy users of gauges). 

Regards pid recycling, I *think* it is only an issue for gauges, which are still deleted anyway in our experiment. Also, given filenames with the pid in should be deleted very soon after the worker death, the chance of a recycling pid clash is very remote? I may have missed something though...

It seems to be working for use, but I would appreciate any feedback on anything we may have missed.

Our experiment involves a slightly altered version of the *MultiProcessCollector.collect()* function, which you can see here: https://github.com/canonical-ols/talisker/blob/master/talisker/metrics.py#L193

The changes are documented there, but for convenience are copied here:

    This almost verbatim from MultiProcessCollector.collect().
    
    The original collects all results in a format designed to be scraped. We
    instead need to collect limited results, in a format that can be written
    back to disk. To facilitate this, this version of collect() preserves label
    ordering, and does not aggregate the histograms.
    
    Specifically, it differs from the original:
    
    1. it takes its files as an argument, rather than hardcoding '*.db'
    2. it does not accumulate histograms
    3. it preserves label order, to facilitate being inserted back into an mmap file.
    
    It needs to be kept up to date with changes to prometheus_client as much as
    possible, or until changes are landed upstream to allow reuse of collect().

We'd like to create a PR that upstreams some of this work, if you think the approach is sensible? Specifically, we'd like to slightly refactor the *collect()* method to support preserving label order, allow for not accumulating histograms and not hardcoding '*.db'. This would allow us to reuse it, rather than reimplement it.

Also, if this approach is deemed worthwhile, then we'd be happy to work on a PR to expand to include this aggregation as appropriate.

Thanks for reading!

I'm not sure I agree on the "completely unexpected" part. Have you never used [defaultdict](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#collections.defaultdict) of the standard library or SQLAlchemy column defaults, both of which take factory functions?

There's a reason factory functions are used in situations like these. First, it's impossible to know what kind of copy is appropriate. Second, sometimes you want a default value that is not static, such as a timestamp. If you have an elegant solution to these issues, I'm happy to consider it.

It's perfectly reasonable to expect the UI to handle null values appropriately when there is the possibility of a missing value. A string, even if empty, would be a very unpleasant surprise if another type entirely is expected (such as an object or array).

I checked the documentation and the tests, it turns out `icontains` lookups only work when doing text based lookups.

    You can still do classic django "contains" lookups as you would normally do for normal text fields if you were looking for a particular string. In this case, the HSTORE field will be converted to text and the lookup will be performed on all the keys and all the values:

```python
Something.objects.create(data={ 'some_key': 'some crazy Value' })

# classic text lookup (look up for occurence of string in all the keys)
Something.objects.filter(data__contains='crazy')
Something.objects.filter(data__contains='some_key')
# classic case insensitive text looup
Something.objects.filter(data__icontains='value')
Something.objects.filter(data__icontains='SOME_KEY')
```

http://djangonauts.github.io/django-hstore/#_python_api

For what it's worth, I've finally fixed this issue on my systems. Servers running Apache with -D SSL_DEFAULT_VHOST displayed the problem. In Gentoo, that flag triggers the inclusion of a vhost definition where the "Listen 443" directive is typically located. For some reason, the LE tool could not find it and added ***another*** such directive, hence causing the "could not bind" error.
Hope this helps.

Depending on the situation, we find it convenient to either use TensorFlow on a single GPU (e.g., define a neural net within an actor) and then use Ray to do data parallel training across multiple GPUs or multiple machines.

It also makes sense to define a one actor per machine and within each actor to use TensorFlow's distributed capabilities to parallelize the neural net computations over all of the GPUs on a single machine (and then to use Ray to handle distribution across multiple machines).

There are still some problems remaining to be solved.
1. `pyarrow.plasma.PlasmaClient` is part of Arrow. Currently I use `AsyncPlasmaClient` as wrapper so it is not very efficient (for example, it uses `wait` for ). A better way is to change Arrow's code.
2. ray's `ObjectID` cannot be treated as a base class. So currently I can only create a new class containing ObjectID.
3. Currently awaitable `ray.put` is implemented by making use of `wait`. `pyarrow.plasma.PlasmaClient`'s `get` is complex and there's no easy ways to create an async version. The easiest way may still be changing the code of Arrow.

- From personal experience, Robert's point regarding slow `import` due to remote functions could become a serious usability problem. I remember similar issues from Julia where compiling packages on import was quite annoying because it led to slow program / notebook starts.

- Would lazy shipping also make the @remote annotation unnecessary? That would significantly simplify usability since I don't have to worry about annotating my functions any more (this is a nice feature of Pywren for me).

try:

   try:
      import sascfg_personal as SAScfg
   except:
      import saspy.sascfg as SAScfg
```
The '[install_loc]/site-package' directory is in the search path, but it stops there, so you need the package name as the next level directory to then find files in the package directory: '[install_loc]/site-package/**saspy**/sascfg_personal.py' .

We should require `memory` to have the same units as the `frame` column, which narrows down its dtypes to integer and floats. Also most of the functions in `tp.motion` expect numbers (integers). For a momentary vibration or camera glitch, time does go on and linking trajectories through such a glitch (with `memory=0`) should still result in broken tracks, in my opinion. That's what the memory function is for right?

Consider one blinking particle and one stable particle, both isolated. If you track this (no memory), you will have broken tracks for the blinking particle. If you now remove the stable particle, you will suddenly have a single track for your blinking particle. This makes linking (in some cases) dependent on other particles that are arbitarily far away. Which is not good, it should be an intrinsic property of the isolated particle.

So I would argue for a default behaviour of `expected_frames` that checks for the minimum difference between `frame` values and inserts empty frames if one frame is missing.

We have thought about adding something like `/patch` endpoint to a collection that would record only the content that is missing.
It looks like your approach sets this up in a more manual way, but just uses the existing functionality!
The downside, is that you can only patch one collection at a time, since only one collection can be the recorder source currently.

If I understand correctly, the intended use case is to set up a new collection to patch only what's missing from an existing collection. An example might:

```
collections:
   patch-coll1:
       sequence:
            - name: coll1
               index_paths: ./collections/coll1/indexes
               archive_paths: ./collections/coll1/archives

             - name: live
                index_paths: $live

recorder:
    source_coll: patch-coll1
    source_filter: live
```

Then, you could create a new automatic collection, say `wb-manager init coll1-patch`
 and using then access `/coll1-patch/record/` would record only what's missing from `coll1`

To combine both, you'd then need to also have something like:

```
    combined:
       sequence:
            - name: coll1
               index_paths: ./collections/coll1/indexes
               archive_paths: ./collections/coll1/archives

             - name: coll1-patch
               index_paths: ./collections/coll1-patch/indexes
               archive_paths: ./collections/coll1-patch/archives
```

Then, you could replay the combined version with `/combined/`
Is this the use case that you're imagining?
Do you anticipate wanting to do this for multiple collection?
If so, the `/patch` endpoint may seem useful.
Do you want to have the patched content be separate from the original collection, as would happen here, or added to that collection?

This is a well presented suggestion, it has a few issues though. I agree that the current `[p]mute server` command is too API spammy. However applying a role instead is a change in behaviour, so this could be a separate command (*cough punish cough*). One use of `[p]mute server` is to quickly restrict a user to a small number of channels by doing `[p]mute server` followed by `[p]unmute channel`, because doing `[p]mute channel` in every other channel would take a long time. Applying a muted role to a person changes the behaviour in that `[p]unmute channel` wouldn't do anything to someone who is server-muted.

An alternate approach might be to allow roles to be muted the same way users can, then mods can just apply a muted role. This avoids changing the behaviour of `[p]mute` but doesn't solve the API spammy nature of the command itself. I'm open to more discussion :)

@pgleeson - ok, took a while but figured what the issue is, although not sure exactly how to solve it:

- problem is the poissonFiringSyns are stochastic so require an associated h.Random -- otherwise output is different (which is what was happening here).

- however, in cell.py the h.Random is only created if `params['originalFormat']=='NeuroML2_stochastic_input'` (https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/cell.py#L1006), which only happens if this condition is met in neuromlFuncs.py `if isinstance(input_comp_obj,neuroml.PoissonFiringSynapse)` (https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/neuromlFuncs.py#L1033), which seems like is never met.

- Also, there seems to be 2 small bugs in https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/cell.py#L1013:
1) self.stims[-1] is accessed before appending the stim (line 1017)
2) ['NeuroML2_stochastic_input_rand'] should start with an 'h' so that it is removed before gathering (otherwise will crash)
Both of these can easily be fixed by replacing with:
`params['hNeuroML2_stochastic_input_rand'] = rand`
(I made this change locally to test it worked but didn't commit)

- I also enforced `format = 'NeuroML2_stochastic_input'` in neuromlFuncs.py to make sure an h.Random was associated to the stim. 

With the above changes the output was identical when using different num of mpi nodes. However, I didn't commit since wasn't sure exactly how you wanted to handle the 'NeuroML2_stochastic_input' in neuromlFuncs.py

Let me know if this makes sense and if you need me to make any changes. thx

@DirectXMan12 I can give some examples using ``krbV``

- Get and store credential using default ccache and keytab.
  - Credential will be stored in default ``/tmp/krb5cc_[uid]``
  - Using Kerberos default keytab ``/etc/krb5.keytab``

  ```
  context = krbV.default_context()
  ccache = context.default_ccache()
  keytab = context.default_keytab()
  pincipal = krbV.Principal('HTTP/hostname@REALM', context=context)
  ccache.init(principal)
  ccache.init_creds_keytab(principal=principal, keytab=keytab)
  ```

- Store credential into non-default ccache file.

  I can construct ccache object instead of calling ``default_ccache``.

  ```
  ccache = krbV.CCache('/tmp/krb5cc_myapp', context=context)
  ```

- Use an application-specific keytab.

  Similar with ``ccache``, I can construct keytab object instead of calling ``default_keytab``.

  ```
  keytab = krbV.Keytab('/etc/httpd/conf/httpd.keytab', context=context)
  ```

- Get credential information from a ccache

  ``CCache`` object provides a method ``get_credentials`` to get information from a ccache file, for example [line 68](https://github.com/krbcontext/krbcontext/blob/master/krbcontext/utils.py#L68).

All these functionalities depends on the krb5 C library functions, that is ``krb5_*``, rather than GSSAPI.

So the idea here is actually to eliminate `numpy.distutils` entirely over time and replace it with scikit-build. We can't do that if we write `f2py -c` because `numpy.distutils` is still used. Instead we want to use `f2py` to generate the C files and then link them to the Python extension.

I'm also not happy with the way that Cython support was implemented in scikit-build. Ideally, you would be able to write the following and have scikit-build autogenerate the cython and f2py targets:

```
add_library(LIBRARY_NAME
    input.pyf.src
    input.pyx
) 
```

If you look at [what we're replacing](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/5531da38cec429f3cf2f9aca0f698e2130562592/scipy/fftpack/setup.py), we essentially need to translate the `add_library` and `add_extension` function calls on a one-to-one basis.

We established that the source of the issue was some new behavior from pickling `super` itself.  I've pinpointed it.  The issue is that pickling a function that contains `super` now produces a `__closure__` that has a `cell` object which has a pointer to an instance of the class.  It's recursive because the instance of the class that is produces is a new instance, unfortunately... and I think python made that decision because `pickle` serializes classes by reference, and that should break the recursion.

```
>>> o = obj()
>>> o.__init__.__func__.__code__.co_names
('super', 'obj', '__init__')
>>> o
<__main__.obj object at 0x10558bd68>
>>> # 3.6
>>> # dill.dumps(o.__init__.__func__, byref=True)  # WORKS
>>> # dill.dumps(o.__init__.__func__)  # RecursionError
>>> o.__init__.__func__.__closure__[0].cell_contents()
<__main__.obj object at 0x1055bd400>
>>> # 2.7
>>> o.__init__.__func__.__closure__
>>> 
```

Therefore, I think a reasonable, but not perfect, solution within `dill` is to detect when `co_names` includes `super`, and then temporarily change the `_byref` flag on the pickler to `_byref = True`.  

With this edit to `dill.dill.save_function`:
```
            if 'super' in obj.__code__.co_names:
                _byref = getattr(pickler, '_byref', None)
                if _byref is not None:
                    pickler._byref = True
            pickler.save_reduce(_create_function, (obj.__code__,
                                globs, obj.__name__,
                                obj.__defaults__, obj.__closure__,
                                obj.__dict__), obj=obj)
            if 'super' in obj.__code__.co_names and _byref is not None:
                pickler._byref = _byref
```
Then this succeeds.
```
import dill

class obj(object):
    def __init__(self):
        super(obj, self).__init__()

repr(dill.dumps(obj(), byref=True))
repr(dill.dumps(obj(), recurse=True))
repr(dill.dumps(obj()))
```

Note that this particularly unusual case will also work, except when `recurse=True`.
```
import dill

class obj(object):
    _super = super
    def __init__(self):
        obj._super(obj, self).__init__()
```
This kind of unusual case fails for both `2.x` and `3.x`, so it needs a bit more investigation before I can say if it's the same issue or not. The workaround for super I tried above is obviously defeated by the above.  There's probably a better fix.  Ultimately, what's needed is once `super` is detected in the function, the "appropriate" objects that are produced by the resulting closure should be serialized with `byref=True`.

The difference is that `ProcessingPool` uses `multiprocess` and `ParallelPool` uses `ppft`.  The former is for process-parallel computing, while the latter can be used across a network connection.  Typically, use `ProcessingPool`, unless you need to do distributed computing.  Also, they serialize differently... `multiprocess` uses `dill` (pickling), while `ppft` uses `dill.source` (extracting the source code).

I believe the issue is that you are shipping `run_call`  but as written not all the variables are defined in scope.  The key is to fully-encapsulate the declaration of all variables. If you rewrite as below (with the import on the inside of `run_call`), it should work.

```
from pathos.pools import ProcessPool, ParallelPool, SerialPool, ThreadPool

def run_call(i):
    from subprocess import Popen, PIPE
    p = Popen('echo hello', stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE, shell=True)
    out,err = p.communicate()
    p.wait()
    return out

def run():
    p=ParallelPool(1)
    res = p.map(run_call, range(1))
    p.close()
    p.join()
    print(res)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    run()
```

Exit of testing is breaking tradition with the unittest family (see below).

Why?

---- 

`unittest`'s runner do not exit on `SystemExit` (nor does `unittest2`): 

```python
# test.py
import unittest

class Test(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_1(self):
        pass

    def test_2(self):
        raise SystemExit(0)

    def test_3(self):
        pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    unittest.main()
```

produces

```sh

.E.
======================================================================
ERROR: test_2 (__main__.Test)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 9, in test_2
    raise SystemExit(0)
SystemExit: 0
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 3 tests in 0.001s
FAILED (errors=1)
$ echo $?
1
```

Okay, so here's a little about what I think the code structure will look like. These sorts of things (proxy objects) all look the same in many respects. The term *proxy object* is just our name for objects like `Paragraph` and `Document` that "wrap" (proxy) an XML element, providing API calls that raise the abstraction level from the `lxml/oxml` level up to something more suitable as a developer interface.

Let's start with the calling protocol I think we're after. This might bear more discussion, but this will do for a start:

```python
>>> document = Document()
>>> bookmark = document.start_bookmark(name='test')
>>> document.add_paragraph('test bookmark body')
<Paragraph object at 0x...>
>>> document.end_bookmark(bookmark)
```

I've explicitly named the parameters, just for clarity. I expect there's no good reason not to allow them to be identified by position, e.g. `.start_bookmark('test')` should be allowed. Later there will also need to be `Paragraph.start_bookmark()` and `Paragraph.end_bookmark()` and perhaps others, all of which can be mixed and matched (start in any, end in same or different ). But this is a good first step, much of which will be reusable or adaptable for the other cases.

So we'd want the code above to produce this XML:

```xml
<w:document>
  <w:body>
    <w:bookmarkStart w:name="test" w:id="1"/>
    <w:p>
      <w:r>
        <w:t>test</w:t>
      </w:r>
    </w:p>
    <w:bookmarkEnd w:id="1"/>
    <w:sectPr w:rsidRPr="0006063C" w:rsidR="00FC693F" w:rsidSect="00034616">
      <w:pgSz w:w="12240" w:h="15840"/>
      <w:pgMar w:top="1440" w:right="1800" w:bottom="1440" w:left="1800" w:header="720" w:footer="720" w:gutter="0"/>
      <w:cols w:space="720"/>
      <w:docGrid w:linePitch="360"/>
    </w:sectPr>
  </w:body>
</w:document>
```

The first challenge is to get the bookmarkStart and End elements to appear *above* the sentinel `w:sectPr` element. It looks like that can be done by adding this line to `CT_Body` in `docx.oxml.document`:

```python
bookmarkStart = ZeroOrMore('w:bookmarkStart', successors=('w:sectPr',))
```

The implementation in `docx.document._Body` would be something like this::

```python
def start_bookmark(self, name):
    bookmarkStart = self._body.add_bookmarkStart(name)
    return Bookmark(bookmarkStart)
```

This leaves some of the work to other objects. In particular, the bookmark id must be resolved and assigned by `CT_Body.add_bookmark()`. There is already an `.add_bookmark()` method on `CT_Body`, placed there by the metaclass as a consequence of the `ZeroOrMore()` call above. That will need to be overridden to give it that further functionality. An alternative would be to use a different name like `.add_bookmarkStart_with_next_id()` or something, but I typically just override. I'd have to look around for another element class that does that to what all needs to go in there.

In addition, I expect there needs to be a `._next_available_bookmark_id` helper property on `CT_Body` that can take care of that little chore. `.add_bookmarkStart()` would automatically assign the next available bookmark id and then it would be available from the `bookmarkStart` element that's passed to the `Bookmark` constructor.

This may be a good place to mention that a proxy object (like `Bookmark`) should have *no state* of its own. All state is in the XML. So, for example, if you wanted to add a property `Bookmark.id`, when called, it would go to the XML to find out and return that value. There would be no `._id` instance variable hanging around to get out of sync with the XML. Its implementation would be something like `return self._element.id`.

Note that `.end_bookmark()` is on `Document`, and that another method of the same name will need to be on `Paragraph` and anywhere else a bookmark can end. Just because it starts in `Document` (under the `w:body` element) doesn't mean it has to end there. But in either case, a bookmark can't end itself. It needs reference to a context element (e.g. `w:body`, `w:paragraph`, etc.) and so the most natural interface I think is on the proxy object for that element (e.g. `Body` or `Paragraph` respectively). I suppose an interface like `Bookmark.start_on(paragraph)` and `Bookmark.end_on(document)` is possible, but it's not screaming out to me so far as a superior design.

Does that give you something to noodle on for next steps?

These are the client rules that need to be enforced to avoid overflows:

1. `T1` and `T2` overflow checks must be enforced by the Raiden clients, and we assume they are properly validated, this implies that `0 <= T1 - T2 <= D1` and `0 <= T2 - T1 <= D2`.
1. The withdraw values W1 and W2 are agreed and validated by the Raiden client, where the constraint `W1 <= A1r` and `W2 <= A2r` must be enforced
1. The lock values `A1l` and `A2l` are agreed and validated by the Raiden client, where the constraint `A1l <= A1a` and `A2l <= A1a` must be enforced.

To enforce rule 1 we do:

- Apply the new deposit only after a number `X` of confirmations blocks [1](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L1376-L1377) [2](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L1308-L1313)
- We then define the available balance as [`A2a = D2 + T1 - T2`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L565-L569), where `T1` and `T2` are updated only by valid balance proofs, meaning `A2a` will vary from `0` to `D2`
- We then define the distributable balance as [`A2d = A2a - L2`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L587-L588)

For sending transfers, we validate the amount is lower or equal to `distributable`.
- [For direct transfers](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L781)
- [For mediated and refund transfers](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L836-L837)

 This is required for two reasons:
1. Sending an invalid balance proof, which tries to use more then the available distributable amount, may trigger the partner node to close the channel, making it short lived.
2. Depending on the transfer type, this may be used to steal tokens. E.g. a direct transfer with a larger transferred amount will ignore the locks.

For receiving transfers, we validate the amount is lower or equal to `distributable`.

- [For direct transfers the transferred amount must have changed by at most distributable](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L270-L283), the [locksroot must not have changed](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L218-L229), nor the [locked amount](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L244-L255)
- [For mediated and refund the lock amount must be <= the distributable amount](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L393-L405), the [locked amount must have increased by the lock.amount](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L368-L378), [the locksroot must include the new lock](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L344-L354) the [transferred amount must not have changed](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/bb53706822a628750118e6215c7ea6c5a12b22e3/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L356-L366)

This is required because:
- Accepting a transfer with an invalid value, even one that benefits the receiver, may result in overflows on chain. Therefor invalid messages must be ignored
- It's a way to identify if a node is trying to play the protocol, which is interpreted as a malicious node

This problem is a bit more generic than just Execute Command / Start Command. Any other functions that require acces to a location that your 'generic' domain account hasn't has this issue.

I've seen it go wrong in:
- Get File
- Get Directory
- List File in Directory
etc.

They all use your default account, and are thus very dificult to use in a sudo heavy environment. My work-around for this is to move/copy the files that are required to be modified/retrieved or adjusted to an area on the remote server where one does have access. Such as /tmp/, then chmod those files and then perform the actions that the SSH library supports.

It'd be much preferred if we were able to provide sudo options with keywords that would otherwise just use your 'normal' given account. (Either that, or this is already possible, and I couldn't figure it out. Still a bit of a beginner in this).

Ideas or thoughts are appreciated. :-)

`--variable BROWSER:HeadlessFirefox` is a very good point. Obviously could also do `--variable BROWSER:Firefox --variable HEADLESS:True` but that's a bit more typing. One benefit of having headless as a separate argument is that if enabling the headless mode is in the future supported also by other browsers by using `options.set_headless()` like Chrome and Firefox apparently do, we didn't need to change the code to support e.g. headless Edge. This would require some changes to the current logic to create drivers, though, but shouldn't be too complicated.

Another approach is supporting `Headless` prefix with all browsers in addition to (or instead of) explicit `headless` argument:

```python
if browser.upper().startswith('HEADLESS'):
    browser = browser[len('HEADLESS'):]
    headless = True
```

Some updates for you guys: I started to stress-test the json-editor using a record with >3000 authors. 

Let's start with the bad news: Chrome on my desktop machine (proper i7 + 8GB + SSD) needs ~35seconds to generate the form. During this time, the site is unusable. On Firefox the situation is even worse. After I went through the json-editor source code I think that it is totally unusable for this task. The data and update handling is way to inefficient (e.g. `O(n^2)` when processing arrays).

The good news: I managed to render the form in <3sec using good old plain JavaScript. And there is room for improvement (e.g. use elements `p` instead of `input` and generate the input fields only on click/access, because input fields seem to be very heavy food for the browser). There are many features missing (e.g. add/sort array elements, change types, collapsing) but I'm already able to retrieve JSON from the form. And the data handling looks good so far. For validation I would try https://github.com/geraintluff/tv4 + lightweight local validation (so you get errors directly when editing fields). 

Btw: I won't use [clusterize.js](https://nexts.github.io/Clusterize.js/) because on my browserS it is slower than a rendered form and is very unsmooth when scrolling. I think up-to-date browers are will work here very well when we are smart enough to provide them the right (KISS-)input.

This is the "env_chk" functionality, more details here:
https://hackingmaterials.github.io/atomate/concepts.html

In essence, the actual FireTask that you are coding will take your desired String for the VASP executable and pass it through a function in atomate called env_chk. The FireTask code itself is running your parameter through env_chk.

* If your String looks like this: ">>something<<", it will replace "something" with the value of your fw_env's "something" key (set by my_fworker.yaml). This allows "something" to change for different machines by setting different values in the different my_fworker.yaml.
* If your String doesn't look like the above, it is just pass-through. So if you set it to "something", it just stays "something". Thus, the env_chk doesn't interfere with hard-coding any parameters you just wanted hard-coded.

Yes, both are different things but the protocol version is linked to the kinto version rather than the cliquet version because kinto is defining an API on top of the cliquet protocol.

If the cliquet protocol changes, then the kinto version and kinto api version will reflect this change on the next update. (because a version of kinto is linked to a specific version of cliquet at release time.)

Also we may want to display:
 - kinto version
 - cliquet version
 - api version

I think I got better reprs mostly working for Images and BaseDeviates now.  I got ambitious though and started going after `LookupTable`s, `SED`s, and `Bandpass`es, which has led down a bit of a rabbit hole.  (Lots of chromatic code relies on `SED.__eq__`, which is currently implemented by comparing reprs.  I also got sidetracked trying to turn ChromaticSum.objlist from a list into a frozenset so that a + b == b + a ..., and similarly convolution).

Two potential points of discussion:
- I think we should ditch the `eval(repr(obj)) == obj` test in `do_pickle`.  Not even bare ndarrays pass this test with the default numpy printoptions (including ones that don't get truncated due to their length):
```
from numpy import array
a = array([3.14159265358979])
b = eval(repr(a))
print a == b # gives [False]
```

- I think `__eq__` and `__hash__` should probably not depend on `__repr__`.  This is especially true if we want to wrap `ndarray.__repr__` in our own `__repr__`s (which maybe we don't?).  The problem here is that ndarray `__repr__` output depends on the current state of numpy printoptions, which can change in the middle of programs, effectively turning immutable objects into mutable ones, at least for the purposes of `__eq__` and `__hash__`.  More to the point though, it's got to be faster to just compare attributes directly than to compare their stringified representations.

I'm happy to take this on, but I thought I'd gather opinions before launching full out `__hash__` warfare.

Oh there is a typo, it should be. `gsutil -m cp -z text *.txt gs://<bucket>`, but more or less the same.

Here is an exerp from running the help dialog that was what I used to figure out what all the flags did.

```
gsutil cp --help


  -z <ext,...>   Applies gzip content-encoding to file uploads with the given
                 extensions. This is useful when uploading files with
                 compressible content (such as .js, .css, or .html files)
                 because it saves network bandwidth and space in Google Cloud
                 Storage, which in turn reduces storage costs.

                 When you specify the -z option, the data from your files is
                 compressed before it is uploaded, but your actual files are
                 left uncompressed on the local disk. The uploaded objects
                 retain the Content-Type and name of the original files but are
                 given a Content-Encoding header with the value "gzip" to
                 indicate that the object data stored are compressed on the
                 Google Cloud Storage servers.

                 For example, the following command:

                   gsutil cp -z html -a public-read cattypes.html gs://mycats

                 will do all of the following:

                 - Upload as the object gs://mycats/cattypes.html (cp command)
                 - Set the Content-Type to text/html (based on file extension)
                 - Compress the data in the file cattypes.html (-z option)
                 - Set the Content-Encoding to gzip (-z option)
                 - Set the ACL to public-read (-a option)
                 - If a user tries to view cattypes.html in a browser, the
                   browser will know to uncompress the data based on the
                   Content-Encoding header, and to render it as HTML based on
                   the Content-Type header.

                 Note that if you download an object with Content-Encoding:gzip
                 gsutil will decompress the content before writing the local
                 file.
```
